coredata.dc:title: Progress in membranes for pressure retarded osmosis application
coredata.dc:creator: Rahman, Sumaita Nawar
Saleem, Haleema
Zaidi, Syed Javaid
coredata.dc:description: The ever-growing global energy crisis has been pushing industries to lean towards renewable resources as an alternative for power generation. Recent research is focusing mainly towards achieving water sustainability and producing green energy without creating any liquid discharge. In this review paper, a complete analysis of the latest developments in membrane modification for pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) processes has been discussed. Moreover, the use of different nanomaterials like zeolite, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, halloysite nanotubes, TiO2, SiO2, silica, carbon quantum dots, covalent organic frameworks, and metal-based nanoparticles for membrane modification to maximize PRO performance have been presented. Besides, the hybrids of PRO in combination with reverse osmosis (PRO-RO), forward osmosis (PRO-FO) and PRO thermal have also been reviewed. Additionally, the recent test results on the incorporation of novel nanofibers like polyetherimide and polyacrylonitrile into different thin film composite and hollow fiber membranes have been presented here. Also, this review presents the future direction of PRO research, economic analysis on cost reduction and its commercialization potentials. Due to its immense benefits offered, the Middle East is beginning to consider PRO process to enhance the shrinking fresh water source in the region, while producing salinity gradient energy.

head.ce:title: Progress in membranes for pressure retarded osmosis application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ever-growing global energy crisis has been pushing industries to lean towards renewable resources as an alternative for power generation. Recent research is focusing mainly towards achieving water sustainability and producing green energy without creating any liquid discharge. In this review paper, a complete analysis of the latest developments in membrane modification for pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) processes has been discussed. Moreover, the use of different nanomaterials like zeolite, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, halloysite nanotubes, TiO2, SiO2, silica, carbon quantum dots, covalent organic frameworks, and metal-based nanoparticles for membrane modification to maximize PRO performance have been presented. Besides, the hybrids of PRO in combination with reverse osmosis (PRO-RO), forward osmosis (PRO-FO) and PRO thermal have also been reviewed. Additionally, the recent test results on the incorporation of novel nanofibers like polyetherimide and polyacrylonitrile into different thin film composite and hollow fiber membranes have been presented here. Also, this review presents the future direction of PRO research, economic analysis on cost reduction and its commercialization potentials. Due to its immense benefits offered, the Middle East is beginning to consider PRO process to enhance the shrinking fresh water source in the region, while producing salinity gradient energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past, several energy sources have been attempted to be utilized in order to provide energy for the world's growing population and to sustain a healthy planet. At present, energy generation mainly depends on fossil fuels, which results in several negative impacts on the environment by generating greenhouse gases. To overcome this issue, scientists are focusing on an alternative source of energy which is renewable, sustainable, and environmentally friendly that does not require any combustion energy [1,2]. Blue energy obtained from the salinity gradient has the potential to be the best alternative method since it's the most promising renewable energy that can bring down pollution to 0 %. Generating power from these salinity gradients, with the help of the PRO process, has been deeply researched for its dependability and reliability. Besides, the association of PRO technology with desalination plants for dilution of highly concentrated brines to convert it to electrical energy adds more value to its promising green solution [3,4,5,6].

PRO technology is comparatively a new and an advanced membrane process that offers innovative solutions to solve the water and energy crisis in the present world [7]. As water is needed for generating energy, desalination is still used as an excellent option for solving the global problem of water shortage [8,4]. However, desalination process comes with large capital costs, hypersaline brine production and high energy requirements which creates various obstacles [9,10]. Many studies have suggested the utilization of renewable energy sources (RES) in desalination sector to reduce its environmental impacts and to minimize the system's large capital cost [11]. Hence, adding PRO membrane systems to desalination is very crucial. Although the growth of PRO membranes has been acknowledged and gained popularity, but it is still under debatable conditions in terms of practical use [12]. PRO process uses osmotic energy along with advanced membranes to generate energy by mixing high salinity and low salinity water streams. This energy is transferred to a turbine attached with the system which in turn produces electrical energy [13]. In the practical world, power can be generated from this difference in the salinity gradient between sea water and river water using this advanced technique of osmosis. The osmotic pressure remains higher than the hydraulic pressure applied in this system, which navigates the transportation of water by the net osmotic force to push the feed solution to the draw solution with the help of a pressurized permeated stream [13]. This pressurized permeate solution is then converted to electrical energy with the help of a turbine. PRO membrane has been preparatorily proven to possess a powerful capacity to obtain continual energy supply from different salinity gradients equipped with highly desirable power densities and drastically improved membrane fouling system [12].

PRO technology uses the natural osmosis process whereas in RO the reverse of osmosis process happens due to the extra pressure applied. RO uses the hydraulic pressure or an energy source to resist and surpass the osmotic pressure of the seawater to generate freshwater [14]. Additionally, in PRO membrane, the osmotic pressure from sea water to mix with fresh water is naturally harvested and then converted to mechanical energy to generate electricity [13]. The recent advancement allows PRO to support RO networks by using the rejected brine (hypersaline solution) from reverse osmosis to be used as a high concentrated solution in PRO membrane systems [15]. Moreover, wastewater retentate and treated sewage can be used for low salinity stream in PRO system. One of the most important factors of PRO productivity is proper pairing of feed and draw solutions, as significant difference in salinities of both solutions is crucial to maintain balance. Higher osmotic power is achieved when there is high salinity difference. Thus, high water flux and higher power density are resulted in extreme salinity differences.

The main objective of this review paper is to analyze different membrane modifications suitable for the PRO process to generate electricity. Moreover, the paper presents the latest developments of membranes and modules for the PRO process, along with several practical implementations of PRO membrane technology. Systematic description of different nanomaterials like zeolite, carbon nanotubes (CNT), multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), SiO2, TiO2, GO, HNT, silica, metal organic frame (MOF), covalent organic frame (COF) and carbon quantum dots (CQD) used in different membrane modification process for PRO process have been carried out. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have discussed the nanomaterial incorporation in membranes for PRO application, and out of which the use of silica, TiO2, and zeolite was not observed in many recent ones. Also, very limited studies have addressed about the ways to overcome the limiting factors for PRO membranes which includes internal concentration polarization (ICP), reverse solute diffusion (RSD) and membrane fouling. This review elaborately described the application of antifouling membranes and different fouling control measures that can be employed in PRO process. Thus, the review aims to add new insight and knowledge for the scientific community in the PRO field, represents related papers based on PRO process and the gap in each study that motivated the formulation of this review paper.

Fig. 1 demonstrates a statistical data that targets the total number of publications based on PRO process which were published in Elsevier since the last decade. Starting from 2011, increasing growth in publication number was observed and the peak was reached in 2016. Year 2016 had the maximum number of publications in Elsevier based on PRO technology. Moreover, from the year 2019 to 2021 gradual increment has been reflected. This indicates that the number of research and publication based on PRO process is expected to increase in the upcoming years. (See Table 1.)

The theory of generating energy from the salt gradient by the mixture of freshwater and saltwater was first stated in a Nature article by Pattle in 1954 [16,17]. Although the paper contained information on how selectively permeable membranes can be used to obtain power with osmotic force, it did not receive much attention during that time. Twenty years later, the topic of PRO technology was brought to light due to a sudden increase in oil prices and numerous inquiries related to the economic practicability of PRO technology were published in 1973 [16,18]. The concept of moving electric turbines by maintaining the same concentration of two fluids with the help of semi-permeable membranes to generate electricity was in fact a very expensive method and it was disregarded. During the 1980s, as fossil fuel became the dominant energy source for generating electricity [19], the concept of PRO became easily forgotten. Thirty-five years later, a power station operating through pressure retarded osmosis was opened in Norway by the company called Statkraft. The prototype of the membrane was 2000 m2 with an efficiency of generating 1–2 W/m2 electricity [20]. Statkraft's plan was to build a fully functioning 25 MW osmotic power plant by 2015, but due to high operating costs the power station was shut down in 2013. Even after several attempts to establish this technology, the development of the PRO process continued and revealed multiple prospects of membrane modification to make the whole PRO process more effective.

In PRO, the working principle is based on water usually passing through a permeate from low to high salinity and pressurized solution. During this process, power is generated by a rotating turbine that turns mechanical energy to electrical energy. In this system, the draw solution makes contact with the active layer and the feed solution touches the support layer of the membrane for proper filtration [21,22]. Moreover, the whole process in PRO technology of transporting water from a low salinity solution to high salinity pressurized solution by permeable membrane to generate power can be taken as the inverse of RO. This is because hydraulic pressure is used in RO to conquer the osmotic pressure of high saline water to produce fresh water. On the other hand, PRO uses osmotic pressure high saline water to stimulate hydraulic pressure [16]. Due to this similarity with RO, many commercially available RO membranes were used by researchers for PRO process for and analyzing its feasibility [12]. The prime development of PRO process took place surrounding two primary factors which are membrane fabrication methods and mathematical models that can replicate the water flux. Many research on membrane fabrication paved the way to achieve high values of power densities which established the practicability of PRO process [23]. Many models were created by scientists since PRO's foundation to commercialize the product and implement it worldwide. The main factors related to PRO technology are power density, water flux and hydraulic pressure. These three elements when combined together generates maximum electricity production potential in PRO membrane. The power density equation of PRO membrane can be stated as: W = J
w
. ∆P; where W is the power density (W/m2), and Jw represents water flux (Lm−2 h−1) and ΔP is the hydraulic pressure (bar) [24]. According to studies, highest power density can be attained when the hydraulic pressure is half the osmotic pressure with a perfect condition. However, the practical works suggested lower results compared to the theoretical values due to the loss of effective osmotic pressure by ICP and reverse solute flux [25]. Therefore, membranes with high osmotic water flux and extreme pressure tolerance were prioritized in order to produce a high-power density from the PRO process to overcome selectivity loss of membrane under pressurized conditions [24].

In general, two main types of membrane are used commonly in PRO membranes, which are integrated skinned membrane developed employing cellulose acetate (CA) and cellulose triacetate (CTA), and the other one is thin-film composite (TFC). Studies suggest that TFC membranes consist of selective layers that have higher water flux than CA or CTA and it also has a porous layer that provides mechanical strength to the membrane. Hence, many researchers have been more inclined towards using a TFC membrane for achieving maximum benefits from PRO technology [26]. Recently, the main advancement of PRO technology was made focusing on membrane fabrication, process optimization, effective module design and commercialization of the technology. Many companies have successfully developed and tested the compatibility of PRO membranes for utilizing it for large scale pilot plants. Out of which, Toyobo Co. is believed to be the most innovative one as it has designed the hollow-fiber forward osmosis membranes that covers a large area of 700 m2 and can function under feed pressure of 30 bar. These membranes are known to be the most suitable for treating salinity gradient of osmotic pressure even when the difference is of 60 bar pressure. Another company names Hydration Technology Innovation (HTI) has recently produced and made saleable flat-sheet cellulose triacetate forward osmosis membranes along with thin-film composite forward osmosis membranes. Recently, Toyobo hollow-fiber membranes were utilized in the Megaton project where salinity gradient resource were used from RO brine that came from tertiary treated wastewater effluent (TSE) [27]. Moreover, another company named Aquaporin also was able to be commercialized hollow-fiber FO biomimetic membranes which enhanced water recycling process with low back diffusion and by just engaging osmotic pressure. Furthermore, natural compounds found in liquids can be concentrated without pressure or heat which preserves the natural and nutritional value [28].

Modelling elements like support layer also went through the different modifications to make it inexpensive to providing better mechanical support for the membranes [29]. Besides, promising outcomes have been observed in membrane modification of PRO technology mainly in hollow fiber membrane, polymeric membrane, thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane and nanofiber supported membranes [30]. Water technology sector has gone through several developments by combining nanostructures and nanocellulose in membrane modification in order to improve its performance [31,32]. Moreover in a recent study, the utilization of organically modified nanoclay in TFC membrane was also observed [33]. Besides water treatment efficiency, the positive and negative impacts of nanomaterials on environment were also detailed in a study by Saleem & Zaidi [34]. A study by Kim et al. observed the antifouling ability in a TFN membrane that was provided due to the carbon nanotube mixed polymeric support for the PRO process [34]. This was a notable progress as TFC membranes are often restricted by severe contamination caused by the placement of the membrane. Moreover, high priority has been given to nanomaterials like SiO2, TiO2, AgO, ZnO, graphene oxide and metal oxides for including in polyamide layer for enhancing the surface hydrophilicity of membranes [14,35]. Recent progress of single and multiwalled carbon nanotubes is also known to enable permeability and fouling resistance to polymeric membranes [6,35]. Furthermore, recent revolution in nanofiber fabrication techniques led to the utilization of nanofiber membranes in different water treatment process [36].

Chemical potential difference on two sides of a membrane generates salt transportation towards a region of low concentration whereas water transport occurs in opposite direction due to the osmotic pressure difference. Therefore, PRO is slightly different from usual reverse osmosis process where salt and water streams in the same direction. This also results in mass transport expressions to vary from each other.

PRO process causes long-lasting fluid flow periods on the permeate/feed side of the membrane that also results in diffusive flow from the membrane surface into the bulk fluid phase. Similarly, the membrane surface concentration also remains greater than that of the phase existing in bulk fluid. However, diffusive flow and convective flow sum must remain the same on the draw side (high concentration) side of the membrane due to the law of mass conservation. This can result in Cp value (concentration at the external membrane interface) to be higher than zero if salt transport happens into the bulk fluid phase. This can depend on concentration of bulk salt and its permeability coefficient.

No other one-dimensional model considers the mass transfer resistance of every transport layer and identifies the solute concentration distribution. The well-established Brian's equation for the water flux developed for RO supports this concept which is, Js = Jw x CP [37].

The water transfer rate determined by the concentration of both sides of a membrane is one of the most critical point of PRO model. For the resolving the value of Jw the concentration of Cm and Csk should be known. Lee et al. have first solved this problem and they obtained for the water flux as [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membranes and its fabrication for PRO process
ce:section.ce:para:
Membrane technology has been aiming towards membrane modification and fabrication for commercializing the most suitable membrane [40]. Several types of membranes used in PRO process are already being researched and also available in the market. Membranes like integrally skinned phase inversion, TFC flat sheet, hollow fiber, polymeric, nanomaterial incorporated TFN membrane, and nanofiber-based membranes have been used in the PRO process. Numerous research has been going on focusing on membrane technology for greater stability and efficiency of PRO process. However, a PRO membrane must be developed with the best traits recognized so far by membrane technology studies. Those characteristics includes:
•
An active layer made with high density for better salt rejection by the membrane.
•
The membrane thickness must be less, involving the least amount of porosity of the support layer for obtaining lower ICP and achieving higher water flux.
•
To reduce membrane fouling, the membrane must have hydrophobicity.
•
The membrane should be robust with high-level mechanical strength to endure hydraulic pressure [41]

For further enhancement of the membrane flux and power density of PRO system, the preferred membrane should have a thin selective layer and the substrate layer. In this way, high power density can be attained in PRO techniques. Besides, for determining the flux and power density of PRO, the thickness and the structure of the substrate also play a vital role. Therefore, it is advised to fabricate a thin substrate to assist in water transportation [42]. Two primary groups of membranes have been developed for PRO process which are flat sheet membranes and hollow fiber membranes which are elaborately discussed in the following section.

Integrally skinned asymmetric (ISA) membranes comprises a thin selective layer and a porous substructure. It is normally made by a non-solvent induced phase separation process (immersion precipitation) [43]. The thin layer is usually supported by multiple sub-layers that are porous in nature. The thin top layer acts as the main selective layer which is prepared with the help of a phase inversion method [43]. On the other hand, phase inversion is a technique that is a combination procedure where the initial homogeneous polymer solution is converted from a liquid state to a solid state under regulated conditions. This technique is the most commonly used procedure for fabricating polymeric membranes, where the choice polymer depends on the structure of the membrane, its properties and chemical interaction [44]. As ISA membranes have low production cost and retain a skinned layer on the top of porous sublayer, therefore most commercial polymeric membranes are made of this material. Moreover, ISA membranes have very thin skin layer that allows high performance, which also facilitates a high permeability together with high selectivity. Hence, a significant part of the membrane market is dominated by ISA membranes [45]. In case of PRO membrane preparation categories, direct phase inversion and IP are the two most commonly used methods [45]. In a recent study, TFC-FO and CTA-FO were used as model membranes where both the membranes have been previously used in FO and PRO studies. Out of both, CTA membrane was an integrally asymmetric membrane that was typically fabricated by a single-step phase-inversion method. Next, these membranes were soaked in deionized water and stored at 4 °C for minimum one day prior to use. After both membranes were run in PRO mode, gypsum scaling was observed in TFC membrane displayed in Fig. 2 In contrast, no gypsum crystal was observed on the CTA active membrane after DI water cleaning [46]. This experiment proved that CTA membrane has an integrally asymmetric structure which is much thicker than TFC membrane. Besides, once the membrane is set in AL-DS orientation, the integrally asymmetric structure can be more resilient to any damage of membrane integrity under harsh scaling conditions due to the thicker active layer [46].

Polymeric membranes with an integrally asymmetric structure can be fabricated easily with a greater potential of up-scaling. However, in recent studies, TFC membranes were more desirable than ISA membranes because of their high polar and nonpolar solvent permeance. Compared to ISA membranes, TFC membranes are not limited to linear and soluble polymers only. ISA membranes are preferable for swelling reduction and for compaction that causes flux reduction [47]. Another experiment was performed to study the potentials of PRO process by using lab-scale different types of fabricated membranes. For this study, much importance was given to hollow fiber (HF) membranes, TFC and integrally skinned asymmetric membranes. When all the membranes were run in PRO mode, results showed that ISA membranes typically worked much poorly than TFC membranes as only 5 W/m2 threshold was met by the membrane. This was due to its thick skin layer and classified pore size distribution in the sublayer. This suggests that TFC membranes are more favoured compared to ISA membranes when it comes to power density. However, it is still argued that the volumetric power density is more significant than power density for utilization of the membrane in large scale practical operations [48]. Additionally, insignificant loss in membrane performance was detected when the membrane was tested in AL-FS orientation. Hence, employing a mechanically high strength ISA with hollow fiber membrane was described to be the ideal membrane when operated in AL-FS operation to overcome fouling issues in PRO process. To add, the results from the predicted performance suggested that if these membranes are successfully scaled up, it can be a promising solution for sustainable PRO operation [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thin film composite flat sheet membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Thin film composite are semipermeable membranes made from two or more layered materials which are used as molecular sieves developed with desired features like enhanced filtration rate, improved salt rejection and greater mechanical strength [23]. Generally, TFC membranes are used in the PRO process because of their high endurance and tolerance towards feed pH. Yet, these membranes are still susceptible to oxidants and chlorine attacks. Several laboratory-based experiments have been conducted on TFC membranes to improve their performance efficiency and durability. TFC membranes are constructed by keeping a selective layer that determines efficiency of the separation and a support layer that provides mechanical strength without creating any flow resistance [50]. The support layer of TFC membrane is usually made of flat sheet which is normally constructed by a non-solvent induced phase inversion (NIPS) technique [51]. On the other hand, the selective layer is arranged by IP process, followed by layer by layer (LBL) assembly. It also goes through multiple other steps like solution casting, dip coating, spin coating, spray coating, and vacuum-assisted filtration methods [50]. TFC membranes prepared by IP method have shown excellent separation performance of ion or molecule [52]. In a study by Tawalbeh et al., three TFC flat sheet membranes were fabricated with different post treatments where the first membrane did not receive any treatment, the second membrane went through 1000 ppm NaOCl For 60 min and then transferred to 0.1 M NaOH solution for 16 h. The third membrane was dripped in 2000 ppm NaOCl for 120 min and 0.1 M NaOH for 62 h. The result from this experiment showed that the second membrane had the highest power density of 9.2 W/m2 that was run by river water feed solution [53]. Similarly, another test was conducted by Han et al. on TFC 200 flat sheet membrane where the first batch received no treatment and the second one was treated with 200 ppm of NaOCl, and the third batch was treated with 600 ppm of NaOCl respectively. The result from this experiment demonstrated that water permeability was increased with higher concentration of NaOCl, but salt rejection was decreased, and low power density was obtained for the first and third batch. However, optimum results were achieved from the second batch that generated power density of 12 W/m2 using 15 bar of hydraulic pressure [54]. Therefore, membranes that had to undergo NaOCl treatment revealed no major changes except for reduced thickness.

Another research group used polyacrylonitrile (PAN) as support layer on TFC flat sheet membrane and stated that power density of 2.60 W/m2 was produced at 10 bar by using 3.5 wt% NaCl draw solution [55]. Several research related to TFC membranes also incorporated nanofibers in the form of advanced electrospun support that showed better performance in withstanding high hydraulic pressure up to 11.5 bar. This nanofiber supported TFC membrane generated a highest power density of nearly 8 W/m2 in working states [56,57]. Lastly, with a thermally readjusted TFC membrane containing a very-thin selective polyamide layer, a considerable declination of ICP was observed and 40 W/m2 of electricity was generated [58]. By comparing commercially available membranes with the laboratory-based modified membranes, it was detected that laboratory-based membranes revealed superior PRO performance and up to 60 W/m2 of electricity can be generated from it [56].

Furthermore, another research was conducted with polyethylene terephthalate nanofibers on TFC membrane with the help of electrospun method. Phase separation was formed with microporous polysulfone layer and IP method was used to produce PA selective layer. Both support layer and active layer has water permeability of 1.13 L m− 2 h− 1 bar− 1 and salt permeability of 0.23 L m− 2 h− 1 with a structural parameter of 651 μm. The use of electrospun fibers in support layer boosted membrane endurance to delamination at high cross-flow velocities because the 340 nm diameter electrospun fibers entangled with the microporous PS layer [59].

TFN model was first introduced in 2007 and it soon took over the RO processes for water purification. This is because the membranes that are formed with nanomaterials have superior absorptivity, selectivity, low fouling tendency and high chemical resistance. The TFN membranes continued to develop over the years, and it was employed in many fields specifically for various separation processes based on aqueous media like nanofiltration, RO and FO processes [60,61,10]. For achieving cost efficiency and reduction of energy footprints, nanoparticles were incorporated with TFC membranes to make them thin film nanocomposite membranes [62]. The growth of TFN membranes flourished during 2012 when a suitable quantity of nanofiller was combined into a thin selective PA layer and as a result modified TFN membranes were produced with improved separation qualities as compared to the traditional TFC membrane [63]. Three dimensional nanomaterials have a lead compared to other particles as it has greater surface area, water assimilation power and better fabrication on the PA layer. Additionally, different tests showed that TFN membranes are more resistant to fouling than that of TFC membranes as it consists of nanomaterials that have higher surface hydrophilicity. The membrane structure is a very substantial feature which influences the fouling mainly due to the structure of porosity which is consistent to the thickness [64]. Although TFN membranes incorporated with nanomaterials have enhanced water flux, but the salt rejection power was not fully improved because of the accumulation of nanomaterials and formation of defects. Focusing on this issue, another class of TFN membrane containing a sublayer of nanomaterials (TFNi) has appeared in current years that showed exceptional progress in water flux almost one scale boost together with improved selectivity. Wide selection of nanomaterials can help in such enhancements such as nano particles, one dimensional or two-dimensional materials and also interfacial coating. This research opened the gate to use nanostructured interlayers as it showed great improvement in the formation of polyamide rejection layers along with elevated water transportation. This modification fulfilled the lacking in membrane permeability and selectivity of TFNi membranes [65]. Several research was carried out to synthesis nanocomposite membranes with outstanding antibiofouling feature for possible application for PRO. Moreover, metal organic framework (MOF) incorporated with TFN membranes were also used for water vapor transport and specially in PRO membrane process. The results showed drastic growth in water transport through the modified membrane [66].

Tubular shaped membranes usually produced by non-solvent induced phase inversion spinning process that provides self-support and bigger surface area is called hollow fiber membranes. In comparison to flat-sheet membranes, they are more robust and were created to overcome breakdowns related to deformation due to the presence of feed spacers. Hollow fiber membranes were developed precisely considering the types of flow and pressure drop factors. Thus, this membrane demonstrates higher performance in terms of water permeability, salt rejection capacity, osmotic driving force, and structural toughness [53]. Hollow fiber membranes consist of thin polymeric tubes, with a diameter of 50–200 μm [67]. These modules have newly drawn significant interest in membrane distillation process because of their high packing density compared to other types of components [68]. After TFC flat sheet membranes, HF membranes are most commonly used in PRO process. Hollow fiber membrane gives smooth flow pattern which decreases the ICP and the requirement for spacer [69]. Besides, hollow fiber membranes used in reverse osmosis can be directly used in PRO processes specially the cellulose triacetate -based hollow fiber membrane as it has excellent permeability performance worldwide. A company name TOYOBO Co. Ltd. in Japan has already commercialized CTA-based RO HF membranes because of their reliability in both PRO and RO processes [70]. Capillary hollow fiber membrane is used as most common industrial membrane because of its very beneficial surface by volume ratio, remarkably small lumen radius and thickness of the membrane. It has membrane coating of a few hundred μm in depth with a porous support layer delivers very favorable mass transport conditions and separation requirements [71]. For high power generation, short and wise hollow fiber modules were preferred. In a study by Tanaka et al., a pilot scale PRO test was conducted with the help of two types of 5-in. scale CTA based hollow fiber membrane components to detect its energy output. It was found that the first module was able to give 10.7 to 15.7 % energy and the second module gave 9.2 to 14.2 % energy recovery efficiently when mechanical efficacy was overlooked [72]. Fig. 3 shows the HF PRO module flow arrangement [73]. The hollow fibers are filled in a structured manner with persistent packing density by closing both ends of packed fibers.

Another type of hollow fiber membrane available are the ISA-HF membranes which are made by the immediate dry-jet wet phase inversion of polymer dopes out of the spinneret and then the coagulant bath [74]. The fabrication process of this type of membrane are straightforward and accessible [73]. However, the limitations in the properties of materials causes the phase-inversion membranes to have a lesser water flux when equated to TFC membranes [74]. Moving to TFC based HF membranes, another noteworthy result was achieved when a polyether sulfone (PES) substrate was used in PRO process. This membrane revealed a power density of 10.6 W/m2 when used with 1 M NaCl as the draw solution and 40 mM NaCl as the feed. The only limitation of the membrane was its low ability to withstand hydraulic pressure which was <10 bar [76]. Later, the fiber strength of this membrane was further improved with stronger material polyether-imide (PEI) as the substrate and the diameter of the fiber was decreased. The cross-sectional structure was also modified to look like finger shaped spongy structure. The altered TFC PRO membrane generated 20.9 W/m2 of power density at a pressure of 15 bar with the same 1 M NaCl as the draw solution and 1 mM NaCl as the feed solution. Fig. 4 , demonstrates the SEM images of the modified membranes. Although hollow fiber membrane was made with highest characteristics like self-mechanical support and easy module fabrication system, but HF is still subjected to membrane fouling [77]. Considering this limitation, several researchers developed innovative membranes for PRO process by membrane modification. In one study, polyether sulfone was used as support layer and PA was used as the active layer to check the performance of the HF membrane. Aiming to increase the mechanical strength of the support layer another polymer called polyethyleneimine polymer was used a year later. This resulted in increased power density and up to 20.9 W/m2 of electricity was generated at 15 bar pressure [53]. This study focused on how HF membranes are well reliant on thickness, smoothness and the kind of material used in active layer.

Lastly, superior power density can be attained in hollow fiber membrane by adding inorganic salts and CaCl2 to the dope solution and by doing some slight modification of the spinning conditions. The renovated operation made the pressure retarded osmosis technology more markable and increased the cost effectiveness of osmotic energy [78].

In membrane technology industry, polymeric membranes are always the first choice as they are very economically feasible. Polymeric membranes are built with greater control of pore size and elevated flexibility with small room needed for installation. The affinity of polymeric membranes towards a particular component and all other characteristics makes them very viable in membrane engineering [79]. Some of the common examples of polymeric membranes are cellulose acetate, polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyimide, polycarbonate (PC), polyethylene (PE), and polypropylene (PP), and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) etc. Polymeric membranes are generally employed in shielding varnishes and paints and acts as barrier properties for packaging films. It is also utilized in gas membrane partition and water purification processes involving reverse osmosis, nanofiltration, ultrafiltration, microfiltration and even in electrodialysis [80]. In a study by Achilli et al., the team used various FO membranes for PRO process, and the polymeric membrane made of asymmetric CTA based flat sheet membranes were examined using DI water as feed and 35 g/L NaCl solution [81]. A maximum power density of 2.73 W/m2 was obtained at 9.72 bar pressure. Moreover, at similar pressure power density of 5.06 W/m2 also attained with 60 g/L NaCl solution and deionized water as feed [82]. Another study was conducted by Pasaoglu & Koyuncu with polyacrylonitrile (PAN) polymer solution where cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were added to it for fabrication process. This study proved that fabricated PRO membranes provide higher resistance to greater pressure while also proving high water flux and salt rejection capacity. Besides, greater PRO water flux of 405.38 LMH was achieved with the new fabricated membrane when deionized water and 1 M NaCl was utilized as feed solution. An increase in 2.10 gMH salt flux was also found which was higher than previous studies. Moreover, the combination of CNC in polymeric membrane made of PAN increased the mechanical strength of the membrane which is a vital phenomenon in PRO applications [83]. Additionally, another type of polymeric membrane called polycarbonate (PC) has been widely used in membrane distillation (MD) processes and currently has been used in MD-PRO process as well. These polymer films are well known for their contact angle and lower thermal conductivity. As known from studies that lower thermal conductivity is directly associated with better energy efficiency of membrane distillation processes including PRO process. Polycarbonate (PC) membranes have demonstrated thermal conductivity of 0.2 W/mK which was lowered compared to other membranes [84]. Other studies also explored the possibility of polycarbonate substrates made via the Loeb-Sourirajan technique for TFC-FO interfacial polymerization of polyamide membranes by comparing their functioning with polyether sulfone supported membranes. It was observed that PC membranes had larger hydrophilic pores, about 172 % larger than those in PES. Nevertheless, having a greater pore size did not aid in higher functionality of PES. Therefore, other tests were ran for achieving smaller pore size for greater performance in PRO membrane process [85].
Table 2 presents the summary of the different membrane and their features as well as comparison analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of RO and FO membranes for PRO process
ce:section.ce:para: In simple terms, RO is known for producing clean water whereas FO can produce clean draw solution. On the other hand, PRO process is used to generate power from the salinity difference [86]. The three processes are often interjoined to achieve maximum efficiency in water technology practices. Many proposals regarding the integration of PRO and FO membranes with RO has been suggested to lower the total energy intake of desalination plants. The technical and economic feasibilities were mainly considered in such combinations for creating an ideal pair [87]. However, the early developmental process of PRO heavily relied on the application of RO membranes. The FO membranes were gradually incorporated with the aim of achieving higher proficiency in PRO systems. Both RO and FO membranes incorporation with PRO has been thoroughly narrated in this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of RO membranes in the early developments of PRO
ce:section.ce:para: Gibbs free energy is produced from two different salinities are mixed, and energy is collected to generate electricity with the help of PRO process. Over the past decades, there has been several research going on PRO membrane to overcome many technical issues for implementation of the technology, but few problems remained unsolved. These mainly revolves around the material used for membrane, fouling of membrane, water movement, process efficiency and its viability [53]. Early development of PRO was done using RO membranes specifically B-10 hollow fibers which had the ability to operate with the applied hydraulic pressure due to thick layer of membrane support. This thick layer excluded the passage of salt ions and lowered the osmotic pressure [88,53]. Numerous findings suggested that a membrane that supports superior water flux, minimal reverse salt flux and has elevated salt rejection capacity is ideal for different PRO applications. Cellulose acetate forward osmosis membrane consisted of all the three characteristics mentioned above and therefore was implemented in early PRO membranes [53,89]. However, FO membranes were soon disabled and replaced with cellulose triacetate RO membranes due to its small thickness, high porosity, and other poor mechanical properties of the FO membranes. Cellulose triacetate membrane is the main constitutes of hollow fiber RO modules in present days for commercial use [90]. Lastly, the hybrid system of PRO-RO or RO-PRO is highly needed when there is a greater requirement for high quality water with reduced salt concentration. Other than that, as higher salinity is generated due to second stage, the seawater gushing into that stage has a lesser salinity but the final one is further suited for greater power requirement [91].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of FO membranes in PRO processes
ce:section.ce:para: Forward osmosis and PRO have been receiving growing interest in osmotically driven membrane practices, as they can provide sustainable solution to water and energy crisis. Both osmosis process functions on different osmotic pressure water bodies that run across a semipermeable membrane. As high pressure is required on the draw solution for PRO membranes, it had a slow and gradual development compared to FO membranes. During PRO operations, most FO membranes had broken down or were severely [92]. Spacers are required for supporting flow channels and for enhancing mass transfer for PRO flat-sheet membrane modules. However, spacers can cause loss of hydraulic pressure along the flow channels as well as distorts the PRO membranes due to operating under high pressure. As a result, drastic increase of reverse salt flux and ICP is observed which significantly reduces both water flux and power density. Furthermore, the burst pressure of flat sheet membranes functions depending on the spacer design [93]. Thus, for the development of effective flat-sheet PRO membrane modules, the detection of spacers such as tricot fabric feed spacers which corresponds to PRO membranes is crucial. On the other hand, PRO hollow fiber modules do not require any spacers as hollow fibers are based on self-supported nature. But distortion of hollow fibers can still take place in PRO hollow fiber modules where two distinct types of deformations are observed under high pressure which can be inner-selective and outer-selective. Therefore, strategical selection is required based on the morphology of membrane and macrovoid distribution must be integrated in the design for generating highly robust PRO hollow fiber membranes [94].

Researchers are aiming to combine both technology and produce a hybrid that can be applied different water treatments, resource retrieval and energy production from membrane technology [95]. RO is an evolving machinery to gather renewable energy using salinity gradient. But PRO process is considerably restricted by serious fouling of membrane if an untreated sewage is employed as the feed solution. To overcome this issue and maximize PRO process, forward osmosis was practically demonstrated as a potential solution to this problem. In an experiment carried out by Cheng et al., FO was used to pretreat the water from wastewater stream that was supposed to be used in PRO process. Consequently, the diluted clean inter-loop solution was used as feed solution in PRO process which considerably reduced membrane fouling tendency in PRO. Therefore, the newly established FO-PRO hybrid system gave birth to continual energy production by osmotic power with advanced properties [96]. The utilization of FO in PRO process successfully gave low fouling possibility, simple membrane maintenance, and low requirement of external energy. Hence, energy consumption footprint was drastically reduced and less impact on environment was caused. Moreover, further data from the experiment suggested that the intensely saline draw solution of PRO can be diluted and sent back to the sea as easy disposal or can be reused. Under this situation, FO retains an equivalent water flux with traditional pretreatment methods using PRO processes. A straightforward project is also presented in order for the incorporation of FO and PRO units [96].

In Fig. 5 , the demonstrated model used a minimal model scheme for the combination of FO and PRO units that shows better performance to fouling and has a water flux of 10–15 LMH. The FO-PRO hybrid can generate a power density of 5 W/m2 when used with 50 % diluted seawater brine. One of the advantages of PRO is it has a subunit role in which power efficiency is less valuable associated to PRO that has a chief unit role as energy utilized in other subunit methods is recaptured as a substitute of generating the extra energy for regular utilization [97]. In addition to that, FO has an economical advantage on water flux as it can be found possibly at a cheaper rate than operating cost in comparison to traditional pretreatment techniques [98]. >5 W/m2 of power density can be reached by inter coiling 1 M of NaCl solution in a FO-PRO hybrid system as the foulants could be efficiently eliminated by FO process, causing only slight membrane fouling. Moreover, the diluted draw solution used in FO can be further used as a clean feed for PRO unit with slight fouling tendency. After that, the draw solution of PRO process can be diluted back and easily disposed which can consequently reduce the post treatment cost for the whole process [99]. To summarize, the reduction of diluted DS in FP in the hydraulic operational pressure required by the freshwater retrieval method provides strength for the FO-PRO system. Nevertheless, high investment is needed for FO process for the membrane space and the salinity grade in PRO process might be insufficient through this model [91].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane modifications with nanomaterials in PRO process
ce:section.ce:para: TFN is a recently developed membrane made by IP method which can be consolidated with nanomaterials within the polyamide layer. Several studies have discussed about the utilization of nanomaterials for different applications along with its environmental impact [100,101]. Different types of nanomaterials are incorporated into polymeric membrane for water treatment process to enhance performance and keep proper balance of permeability. Nanoparticles like TiO2, Al2O3, ZrO2, SiO2, ZnO, AgO, consisting of metal oxides and metals are generally induced into PA membranes for increasing surface hydrophilicity of the membranes [102,33]. Secondly, one dimensional nanotube like carbon nanotubes, multiwalled carbon nanotubes and titanic nanotubes are known to assist in higher permeability and fight against membrane fouling. Lastly, three dimensional nanomaterials are well known for improving hydrophilicity and also enhance the density of charge on the membrane surface. As they are formed with cage like structures that are highly porous, the separation efficiency of TFN membranes can be improved with 1D/2D and 3D nanomaterials [42]. Remarkable changes and major growth of membrane technology has been observed over recent decades which is gradually aiming to take over in the field of sustainable research for novel membrane production. Some two dimensional nanomaterials incorporation helped the PRO membranes for achieving anti-biofouling feature through fabrication [103]. Some of these fabricated membrane displayed enhanced water permeability in PRO test mode along with excellent biofouling property [104]. Most recently, different nanomaterials have been incorporated with polymer-based modified membranes like polyethersulfone, polyetherimide, polyamide-imide for lowering membrane structural distortion while keeping the water flux high under enhanced-pressure for PRO process [56]. The function of different nanomaterials like zeolite, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, multiwalled carbon nanotubes, halloysite nanotubes (HNT), TiO2, SiO2, silica, carbon quantum dots, covalent organic frame (COF), and metal-based nanoparticles have been presented in Table 3 .

Zeolites are a type of nanomaterial best known for having massive potential in separation processes due to their unique structures and properties. Zeolites can act as highly effective catalysts, adsorbents, and ion exchangers in petrochemical industries, and it is widely used in day-to-day life. Besides, zeolites are recently applied for sustainable projects and found to be reliable material for renewable energy and environmental fields for improvement in biomass conversion, fuel cells, thermal energy storage and many more. Additionally, zeolites can be used for CO2 capturing and conversion, for air-pollution remediation, water purification and many more [105]. Frequent research has been conducted for improving the basic and standard membrane properties by the addition of zeolites. Membranes incorporated with zeolites can be classified into three groups which are the self-supported zeolite membranes, the inorganic-supported zeolite membranes and zeolites mixed into a polymeric membrane matrix [106]. In a study by Salehi et al., zeolite nanoparticles were used to modify microporous substrates that can be used for composite membrane. The aim of the research was to reduce ICP that affects membranes efficiency during different osmotic application. 0–0.6 wt% of zeolite nanocomposite substrate was embedded with polyether-sulfone with the help of phase inversion technique. This resulted in increased surface porosity and lowered the structural parameter (S) from 0.78 to 0.48 mm [107]. TFN membrane formed with zeolite substrate also optimized the water flux compared to the basic TFN membranes in PRO test. Moreover, the salt rejection value of 94.7 % was recorded along with 1.93 L/m2 h bar of water flux which was 43 % greater than the normal TFC membranes without any modifications. Hence, the zeolite fabricated TFN membrane was found to have increased water flux which is related to reduction of structural parameter, and which consequently leads to low ICP effect [107].

A study by Shadravan et al. reported the fabrication of a PA thin film on the PS substrate through the IP process. Before forming the thin film, the substance was incorporated with zeolite particles which acted as hydrophilic fillers. The resultant TFNs were characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM), atomic force spectroscopy (AFM) and contact angle analyses to determine different physical characteristics of the membrane. Additionally, the performance of membrane was also tested based on power generation ability. As the power density of PRO is mostly rely on pressure applied and water flux of the system, the machinal stability at high pressure creates a concern for PRO membranes. The final results from this experiment demonstrated that the hydrophilicity and permeability of 3D zeolites aided the permeability, selectivity, and fouling resistance of ultrafiltration. In addition to that when zeolite is integrated in TFN polyamide layer increases the surface properties of the membrane for PRO applications [108]. The surface morphology of TFC and zeolite combined TFN membranes demonstrated a smooth polyamide separation layer. The modified membrane displayed the clear distribution of zeolite on the surface of TFN membranes that indicated its existence on the top of the PA layer. This was achievable as zeolite particles were attached with hydrogen bonding between the surface hydroxyl (-O-) group of zeolites and the carbonyl group of the polyamide layer. The steric hampering of the zeolite in aqueous solution reduced as a result of diffusion of PA layer which took place during the IP process. Thus, the formation of the PA layer was slowed down. Besides, the crumples occurred on the surface layer were also diminished because of the presence of zeolite on TFN membranes. Moreover, the hinderance effect of zeolite nanoparticles flakes and the late interfacial polymerization happened due to excess aqueous monomer's concentration. By this means, the interaction between the nanoparticles and the active layer of the TFN matrix increased.

A recent study was conducted by Shadravan et al. with nanomaterial called zeolite for PRO application, which was incorporated with TFN membrane to investigate its performance. This was done based on a finding that suggested the performance improvement of TFN membrane when nanomaterials were used in the coating or in the active layer also known as PA layer in PRO process. The result of this study demonstrated that TFN membranes when used with nanomaterials reveals higher water flux and high-power density compared to conventional TFC membranes. When the tests were run under different condition for the specific TFN membrane, it was found to generate between the 1.7–38.0 W/m2. Additionally, other factors like draw and feed solution and operation pressure were also involved in such superior function of the membrane in PRO process [108].

Carbon nanocomposites have added extensive attention in different fields like environmental engineering, chemical and sustainable development areas for having outstanding operational, mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical properties [109]. Several research has proved the application of carbon nanotubes in energy storage and electrochemical supercapacitor sector. Besides, CNTs can also be utilized as transistors, nanoprobes, sensors, composite material, templates and field-emitting devices as well [110].

The assimilation of macroscopic thin film carbon materials like CNTs and CNFs forms carbon nanopapers, which is demonstrates outstanding mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties which are highly useful for engineering purposes [111]. It consists of cylindrical shaped projected structures which are comprised of multiple layers of graphene and they are mainly formed of molecular carbon allotropes [112,113]. They are most promising materials in nanotechnology that possess outstanding chemical, electronical, mechanical, and optical properties as it is produced under chemical vapor deposition. CNTs are mainly used as an addition to polymeric composition for improving the thermal stability, hardness, and electrical conductivity of the membrane [114]. Two types of carbon nanotubes are produced based on single-walled CNTs(SWCNTs) and multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) organized and arranged concentrically [115]. The tubular structures are formed by carbon atoms coming together and making a C-C (single bond). CNTs are anticipated to have very strong bonds along with greater Young's modulus in their axial direction. Functionalized CNTs are packed with improved membrane hydrophilicity, porosity, and the negative membrane charge which leads to enhanced membrane functioning mode in PRO process. Carbon nanotube-incorporated support layer for TFNC membranes can be widely used in PRO mode as it shows increased fouling resistance and selective permeability [56]. Nevertheless, the cost of production was found to be the main obstacle for expanding the commercialization of CNTs as it increased the material cost. However, the researchers of Dayton university in 2009 found that nanotube production costs had fallen 100 folds since 1990. Hence, the costing price issue is predicted to be solved in the future [116].

Many studies suggested the presence of advanced absorbency and hydrophilicity made by fCNTs and the chemical engraving of the PA selective layer for further increase in water flux. The study reported an increase of 110.0 % power density when modified TNF membranes with DI and artificial seawater (0.5M sodium chloride) was used as the feed and draw solution respectively. Polymeric based membranes when modified with CNTs can demonstrate maximum membrane efficiency at a reasonable cost as they are well known to increase hydrophilicity and the porosity of the support layer that ultimately results in improved power density [117]. A recent study stated the integration of CNTs in the active layer of TFC membranes to boost the partition performance and antibiofouling impact on the active layer for the FO or RO which can also be used in PRO process. In Fig. 6 , during the process of interfacial polymerization or additional coating, the fCNT was implanted in the PA thin film. The results were analyzed based on anti-fouling potential of the membrane which can be widely used for further PRO applications [34].

Moreover, two or more nested single-wall carbon nanotubes are combined together to form a multi-wall carbon nanotube (MWCNT). The combination process to form MWCNT usually requires a weak intermolecular force or a single sheet-like graphene rolled along up several times around a cylindrical hollow. MWCNT are usually more complex than the single walled carbon nanotubes and this is because of how MWCNT are arranged with successive tubes in increasing diameter (Abdallah et al., 2020). Larger tubes are produced from a single one as multiple number of walls usually from two layers to several layers are formed in MWCNT depending on the usage [118].

In a study carried out by Y. Kim et al., CM-95 multiwalled carbon nanotubes were combined with the support layer of TFN membrane. It was modified with CNT functionalization materials like nitric acid (70 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and sulfuric acid (95.0–98.0 %, Sigma-Aldrich). Different concentration of functionalized MWCNTs starting from 0 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, up to 2 wt% of PES were blended with fabricated membranes. The support layer was modified with polyether sulfone. The experiment was run in PRO mode and the placement of the TFC membrane was done accordingly. In this process, the fouling materials were accumulated on the underside side of the support layer which was distinguished by SEM and AFM. Besides, contact angle measurements and zeta potential analysis of the support layer of the TFN membrane was also considered. Furthermore, functional groups were blended with functionalized MWCNTs and FTIR analysis was conducted. The results demonstrated that 0.5 wt% functionalized MWCNT-blended support layer exhibited peaks of –OH (∼3440 cm−1) and (–COOH) (∼1380 cm−1) [34]. This is because the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups of the functionalized CNTs showed expanded dispersion of CNTs in the polymeric support due to hydrogen bonds present between PES sulfonic groups. Intensity was increased in the modified functionalized fCNTs composite TFN membranes when compared to traditional CNTs. Additionally, a significant boost in water flux was seen as the water flux value for CNT0.5 membrane raised from 7.09 L m−2 h−1 (LMH) to 7.32 LMH due to effective osmotic difference (Δπeff) [34].

A study by Goh et al. used 1.0 g of the purified multi-walled CNTs and added 200 mL of HNO3 to it. The modified membrane was left for refluxed at 150 °C for 6 h. After filtering the resulted dispersion, the residue from MWCNT was rinsed with 500 mL of DI water and 1 L of 0.5 M NaOH solution was added to it until a pH of ~6.0 was achieved. The membrane went through a final rinsing twice by with 200 mL of ethanol. Rinsing procedure was conducted to eliminate the excess acid, oxidation waste and miniature carbonaceous materials generated during functionalization on the multi-walled CNTs. The functionalized multi-walled CNTs (f-CNTs) acquired were put down to soak up at 60 °C for overnight [119].

Fig. 7 displays the TEM micrograph of multi-walled CNTs that reveals the average length of 10 μm and a diameter of 11 nm before dispersion by a probe sonicator. After the process was done, functionalized CNTs were shortened and opened tips of the multi-walled CNTs were observed. Moreover, the expanded version of the image (B) shows formation of a hollow structure with an open tip on multi-walled CNT [120].

In addition to that, structural parameters (S) are drastically reduced, and elevated technical strength is achieved when f-CNTs are integrated with membranes. Scientists have given an estimate of peak density up to 17.3 W/m2 at a hydraulic pressure of 16.9 bar could be reached with 1.0 M NaCl as draw solution and deionized water is used for feed solution when CNTs are integrated with membranes [121]. A critical obstacle for prolonged membrane operations in TFN membranes is the biofouling. In another study, CNTS blended polymeric support layer was used in a TFN membrane to examine the pertinence of this membrane and antibiofouling effect of the membrane for the PRO process. The bottom of the support layer was intensely examined with by SEM, FTIR, and AFM, along with contact angle measurements and zeta potential assessment. Additionally, bacterial adhesion tests on the bottom side of the support layer and biofouling tests in a crossflow operation system was conducted on the membrane functioning in the PRO mode. The results showed improved anti-biofouling characteristics of the underneath surface of the support layer of the TFN membrane having 0.5 wt% of (CNTs) compared to traditional TFN membranes. This was possible due to high negative surface charge, smoothness, and hydrophilicity of the modified TFN membranes. In comparison to bare TFC membrane, almost 35 % decrease in bacterial attachment was observed along with ∼10 % less water flux was observed of the 0.5 CNT-TFN membrane than in contrast with normal TFC membrane in the PRO form [34].

Silicon dioxide nanoparticles are well known for having porous structure, area for high surface activity, and enhanced adsorption capacity, which makes them ideal for developing different membranes used in water treatment processes [122]. Silica is highly applied in environmental fields like for enhancing oil recovery, to reduce brine discharge, for eliminating heavy metals and radioactive compounds into water and for water purification [123]. In a recent study by Niksefat et al., advanced type of TFN membrane was formed by incorporating SiO2 nanoparticles into PA active layer. In this experimentation, 10.0 mM sodium chloride solution was used as feed solution and 2.0 M sodium chloride solution was used as draw solution for evaluating the resulting membrane's effectiveness in functioning for FO [124]. The results showed that, water flux of TFN membrane was raised from 15.50 to 36.50 L/(m2h) when the concentration of SiO2 was raised from 0.0 to 0.1 % (w/v). Another study was conducted by Song et al. based on PRO mode after running several tests on FO membranes. In this experiment, nanofiber support membrane (NSM) was strengthened by nano-SiO2 incorporated with PAN. The SiO2/PAN composite nanofiber membranes were manufactured by electrospinning method with a SiO2 precursor in presence of an acid catalysts [125]. The mechanical strength of this SiO2 based modified membrane was assessed by providing a tensile strength assessment. The outcomes demonstrated that the strain at break was enhanced drastically from 9 ± 3 MPa (NSM-1) to 17 ± 2 MPa (NSM-2), which relates to increased mechanical strength of the membrane. This enhancement could be described by the attraction force of absorption between the SiO2 nanoparticles that were bounded to the PAN polymer chains [125]. The SiO2 nanoparticles looked dull compared to the mass PAN polymer phase, suggesting that the nanoparticles were comprised of heavy elements (i.e. Si) than that of the PAN polymer (C, N, O). Besides, the FESEM image displayed spherical and oval form of particles indicated the presence of SiO2 particulate matters observed in accordance with TEM picture.

Silica or mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) are known as hollow-based nanoparticles that has an appropriate size with a large pore volume and surface area. Silica nanoparticles have pores that ranges from 2 nm- 20 nm in size which gives them large surface area (> 1000 m2 g− 1), better pore volume and superior chemical stability which can be applied in many engineering fields [126]. A study was conducted by Daer et al. with SNPs at various concentrations (0, 0.5, 1 and 2 wt%) with the combination of a chemically and thermally steady polymer Polybenzimidazole (PBI). The polymer is known to be used previously in FO for seawater desalination in areas with elevated seawater temperatures and salinities. In the experiment, flat sheet membranes were manufactured with the help of NIPS method with PBI dope solution incorporated with SNPs. The PBI based silicon nanoparticle modified membrane went through different tests to check the impact of draw solution concentration, crossflow velocity and membrane cell placement. Results demonstrated that the performance of the PBI/SNP membranes upgraded when matched to the original and unmodified PBI membrane. The membrane's structural parameter (S) was reduced when 0.5 wt% of SNPs were added to PBI membranes (S0.5). Moreover, when compared to the original PBI membrane (S0) the augmented the tensile strength was tested to be high with a difference of 31.9 MPa vs. 27.3 MPa and a considerable amount of increased water flux was observed as it went from 7.4 Lm−2 h−1 to 16.9 Lm−2 h−1 [127]. Fig. 8 , shows silica based (SO) modified membrane and different layers of it captured under SEM.

Another membrane modification process was conducted by Sabetahd Jahromi & Mehrfar with silica nanoparticles by using 20 % of silica mixed with a solution of 0.055 mol/lit ion concentration of Na+ to make the selectivity of the membrane as 98.4 % total. However, without the presence of nanoparticles the selectivity of the matrix is 82 %. The findings revealed that the effectiveness of the modified membranes improved to 11 % because of the utilization of silica nanoparticles on the anionic membrane of the membrane design. This experiment also proved that water alkalinity can be reduced through chemical reactions with calcium hydroxide (Ca (OH)2) in presence of silica nanoparticles. The modified membrane also prevented the deposition of particles and increased the antifouling properties of the membrane which ultimately resulted in long persistence of ions' flux. Besides, silica nanoparticles have the ability to give any membrane high thermal stability and mechanical strength [128]. For membranes modified with silica has shown enhanced water capacity, absorptivity, selectivity, and most importantly no environmental emission was reported. Furthermore, membrane's selectivity can be increased by increasing the concentration of silica nanoparticles [128].

Titanium dioxides are a type of nanoparticles present in the form of anatase which have been used and have received substantial consideration in the construction industry in recent years. TiO2 has undergone thorough different studies and was employed in different sectors as catalyst for photocatalysis and as antibacterial agents. Besides, nano paint also known as self-cleaning was done using TiO2 in civil engineering field as well [129]. This is due to their ability to add innovative functionalities to infrastructures, having self-cleaning properties and the capability to eliminate air pollutants through photocatalysis [130]. TiO2 is commonly found as a white unscented powder which is not combustible and has a molecular weight of 79.9 g/mol [131]. In a study by Javadi Azad et al., computational fluid dynamics (CFD) was analyzed using FO and PRO by loading the permeable membrane with TiO2 nanoparticles. This study was conducted to analyze to check if titanium dioxide nanoparticles used has any significant effect on water flux, salt rejection, chlorine resistance, and anti-fouling properties of TFN membranes. However, the main goal of CFD model was to determine the parameters which affects the membrane water flux and to conventional efficiency of the membrane in PRO mode [132]. The experiment was conducted taking one TFC traditional membrane as control and slowly incorporating TiO2 nanoparticles in TFN membranes starting from TFN 0.5, TFN 0.75 and TFN 1. The modified membranes were applied in both FO and PRO processes for conducting a comparative study. The effect of TiO2 on diffusion coefficient in FO and PRO processes was analyzed by using two types of draw solution 0.5 M NaCl and 2 M NaCl against one constant feed solution of 10 mM NaCl. The results were obtained after running the test for both FO and PRO process. From the results of PRO, it was observed that different values of TiO2 showed different water flux values and the flux enhancement tendencies were similar to FO process. But as the initial diffusion coefficient was higher in PRO mode, the water flux was greater in PRO process compared to FO process in all simulations. The average flux of 1.75 × 10−4 mol/m2.s was obtained from 10 mM NaCl, as the feed solution, and 0.5 M NaCl as draw solution in case of PRO process. on the other hand, 1.57 × 10−4 mol/m2.s water flux was achieved for FO process. This implies that the water flux boosted by about 14 % in PRO process. The results revealed that by increasing the stacking of TiO2 nanoparticles, water flux was obtained in PRO process can be boosted. From the results, the highest water flux was detected for TFN 1 was when 10 mM NaCl was used as the feed solution, and 0.5 M NaCl, was used as draw solution [132]. Therefore, cross linking membrane can be obtained by adding TiO2 nanoparticles to polymers used in PRO and FO membranes. Finally, water flux can be increased by loading TiO2 nanoparticles and the hydrophilic properties of TiO2 nanoparticles can improve water permeation through membranes [132]. Moreover, organic fouling resistance was demonstrated in another study by J. Kim et al., where the membrane was coated with TiO2 nanoparticles [133]. Additionally, the coated layer had good robustness against crossflow in PRO operations.

Graphene oxide possesses exceptional traits of nanomaterial with two-dimensional single layer nanosheets that has thickness of 1–2 nm. Several fields like formation of nanocomposite materials, polymer composite materials, for energy storage and biomedical applications required the utilization of GO [134]. GO has hydrophilic functional groups that enhances the antifouling tendency, excellent hydrophilicity, and improved structural features [135]. Graphene oxide sheets were previously used for FO applications to modify the membrane performance and to achieve highest water flux using different draw solutes in desalination processes [136]. Although there has been various research works carried out for the enhancement of TFC membranes with nanomaterials in forward osmosis, more emphasis should be given towards development of TFC PRO membranes mixed with nanomaterials. A recent study by M. J. Park et al. investigated the results from utilization of hollow fiber PES support structure altered by the accumulation of hydrophilic GO nanosheets for increasing the osmotic power. In short, high functioning TFC hollow fiber membranes were developed by integration of hydrophilic GO nanosheets in the PES support layer for PRO uses. The alteration of hollow fiber substrates with GO was made for improving the performance of water flux in PRO process. High freshwater permeability, advanced structural morphologies, and hydrophilicity within the support layer was observed in membranes incorporated with graphene oxide (≤0.20wt%) with polyether sulphone hollow fiber supports. Moreover, no decline in the mechanical strength was noticed in this experiment. Under the condition of 1M NaCl used as draw solution and DI water as feed solutions, a highest power density of about 14.60W/m2 was achieved at 16.5bar operating pressure [137]. In addition to this, another study by Idris et al. focused on PRO process by developing a novel flat sheet TFN for enrichment of osmotic power production by incorporating lab based synthesized GO into the polysulfone polymer matrix. In this experiment five membranes were used having different weight percent of GO (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 wt%) respectively. All the membranes were fabricated in the presence of polyethylene glycol (PEG), an agent that forms pore was utilized in phase inversion method. The final results from this experiment showed that TFN-0.25GO membrane was the best membrane out of all. This specific membrane displayed exceptional water flux, salt reverse flux and elevated porosity. In addition to that, the enhancement in microvoids morphology was observed when compared to the control membrane. TFN-0.25 GO also generated the highest power density among all other membranes. A power density of 8.36 W/m2 at pressure > 15 bar was achieved by the membrane. This paper revealed the advantages of incorporating of GO into the polymer matrix as it drastically heightened the fundamental and mechanical features of the membrane [138]. Moreover, TFC-PES hollow fiber membrane which was modified with GO revealed a power density of 14.6 Wm−2 and water permeability of L m−2 h−1 bar−1. This report showed a strong improvement in water permeability by the incorporation of GO [42]. However, it has to be noted that the cost of commercially available graphene oxide is very high and thus it is very difficult to use it in different water treatment areas. Therefore, scaled-up production of graphene oxide at reasonable price without compromising the quality is a challenging task [139].

Another study by Idris et al., used graphene oxide by the NIPS method to fabricate GO based membranes. In this study, various weight percentage of GO (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 wt%) were loaded and evenly distributed in NMP by ultra-sonication at (40 ± 1)°C for 10 h. After this process, vacuum desiccator was on the dope solutions to eliminate air and dampness prior to membranes fabrication. The standardized dope solution was uniformly casted throughout the glass plate at a precise width of 200 μm utilizing a stainless-steel casting knife and was immersed directly into a non-solvent bath having deionized water. After five minutes, the phase-inversion immersion precipitation process was allowed on the membrane. The results of FTIR analysis from this experiment showed the declination of transmittance with the addition of GO in TFN-GO membranes [138]. On contrary, from another experiment a noticeable transmission rate was observed in TFN-0.25 GO compared to other membrane. However, when the percentage of GO was increased to 0.5 and 1.0 wt%, greater viscosity of the dope solution reduced the rate of phase separation [140]. Therefore, it implies that the structural composition of TFN membranes relies considerably on the amount of GO added which signifies GO as an important nanomaterial for efficient membrane process. Moreover, GO membranes are well known for having efficient power density in PRO mode. As per another study conducted by Tong et al., a high-power density of 24.62 was achieved. In this experiment, 6.90 bar of hydraulic pressure was applied and 0.017 M of NaCl was used as feed solution against 3 M NaCl of draw solution [141]. However, the progress of engineered osmosis processes like PRO often faces high obstacles due to severe irreversible fouling problems which piles up inside the porous membrane support of GO modified membranes. But a recent study conducted by Hu et al. proved the efficacy of dense GO barrier layer formed on the porous side of asymmetric membrane for controlling fouling issues. The results from this experiment further showed effective restoration of water flux of GO membranes by physical cleaning which lowered the fouling level to its initial prefouling stage [142].

HNT is comprised of alumina-silicate clay and has structure similar to micro hollow and nano tubes and it can be chemically comparable to Kaolinite. HNTs are used in various fields because of their tubular shape that makes it suitable for drug carrier, for catalyst and as filler for polymeric matrices [143]. HNTs are used to prepare nanocomposites using different fabrication methods to enhance the physical and chemical properties of membranes to be utilized in various membrane technologies [144]. Halloysite nanotubes can also act as nucleating agent for poly-glycolic acid (PGA) fibers to increase the crystallinity of it [145]. In one study, TFC membrane having double layer of nanocomposite substrate was fabricated using double-blade casting technique for the PRO process [146]. In this experiment, HNTs were integrated within the bottommost polymeric substrate layer and another nanomaterial called GO was on the uppermost layer. A very thin selective PA layer was developed on the upper layer of the substrate. Fig. 9 represents the theoretical model of the based on halloysite nanotubes and graphene oxide nanoparticles structured as double-layered thin-film composite PRO membranes. The manufactured new membrane proved to have excellent membrane substrate properties like open-bottom surface and superior porosity. Besides, it consists of suitable uppermost-skin surface composition for upcoming active layer development. The mechanical strength and other properties have showed significant development that have been extremely efficient for PRO processes.

TFN membrane GO & HNT displayed a water permeability of 2.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and a power density of 16.7 W/m2. This proved that the manufactured membrane obtained high power density and better mechanical power than industrial PRO membrane [42].
Metal based nanoparticle incorporated membranes are mainly formed of different metal oxides or metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) which are purely made of metal-based materials.

Nano metal oxides are utilized for removing toxic chemicals from contaminated water coming from industrial sources. Besides, MONPs are used as catalyst for producing organic materials through reactions and used as heterogeneous photocatalysts for environmental remediation. MONPs can be formed as VOCs and act as biological and chemical sensors as well [147].

Metal oxides nanoparticles (MONPs) have special ability to form wide range of oxide compounds and can be implemented in a vast number of structural geometries with an electronic form that can show the characteristics of insulator, semiconductor, or metallic substrate.
