Metal based nanoparticles are well known for showing distinctive opto-electrical features because of their well-recognized identified exterior plasmon resonance qualities. In the recent state of membrane technology the materials, aspect, size, and shape of the synthesis of metal nanoparticles holds considerable importance [148]. In case of PRO membrane, instantaneous increase in mechanical stability and power density is highly preferred. Therefore, several membranes are known to be assigned in membrane fabrication with the help of a novel substrates by the ceramic-based materials or high strength metals [149]. Out of which, ZnO is a promising metal oxide-based material that has great potential in electromechanical nanoscale production. It is well known in metal oxide group for having high electrochemical stability, super oxidative capability, and low toxicity. ZnO is also the first and most frequently used material for mixed photocatalysis among other metal oxides. The unique positive Zn surfaces and negative O surfaces generates electronic dipoles that permit better polarization of voltage and temperature in particular routes and planes [148].

Some studies also focused on metal organic framework (MOF) where membranes are fabricated by linking inorganic and organic units by strong bonds also known as reticular synthesis. >20,000 different MOFs being stated and examined within the past few years. The main emphasis was on the areas like generating novel PVA/MOF nanofibers, synthesis of amine-functionalized MOFs and many other properties of metal organic frameworks that can be used in membrane technology [150]. Another research by Al-Maythalony has stated the enrichment of CO2 separation choices in MOF based membranes because of its availability of wide variety of structural and chemical diversity. However, more work is required for its implementation in industrial applications as MOF membranes are vulnerable to multiple challenges related to mechanical resilience and high quantity production. Even so, membrane technology based on MOF is valuable and this field is receiving vast interest with huge chances for high-functioning success in the future [151].

Covalent organic frameworks are considered to be best absorbent material for being highly porous, having modifiable characteristics and exceptional steadiness of framework [152]. COFs have displayed favorable viewpoints in energy applications that involves photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, metal-ion/sulfur batteries, etc. Surface COFs are formed by reversible covalent bonding that leaves a room for self-healing and error rectification [153]. Different developed features and special design functionalities has been added to COFs synthesis process [153]. Recently Schiff bases coupling are successfully used for the synthesis of well-defined surface COFs. Schiff bases are also known as imines that are manufactured by condensation reaction of aromatic amines with aldehyde amines and ketone derivatives [154]. As boron oxygen bond of COFs is unstable in water, the imine bonding with COFs essentially solves the problem. A great improvement in structural steadiness of materials has also been observed by Schiff bases addition to COFs. Moreover, due to the unsaturated nature of imine bond, the additional modifications and functionalization can be done easily [155]. COFs are also incorporated in osmotic power generation specifically PRO processes for enhancing water permeability and improving hydrophilicity. A recent study by Gonzales et al. demonstrated the significance of COF nanomaterials as a possible method in PRO membrane fabrication to improve both energy producing capacity and the osmotic performance of the PRO process. An experiment was conducted by using 1 M NaCl as draw solution and DI water as feed solution. A melamine-based COF nanomaterial, Schiff base network-1 (SNW-1), was incorporated into the PA layer of a novel TFN-PRO membrane. The modified layer had 0.020 wt% nanomaterial concentration which demonstrated a power density of 12.10 W/m2 and the greatest water flux of 42.50 Lm−2 h−1. Almost 20.0 bar hydraulic pressure was applied in this experiment [26]. Moreover, FE-SEM analysis was conducted to ascertain the size and characterize the synthesized SNW-1 where the shape and size of SNW-1 particles were observed. The small size of the particles showed improvement in the process of homogeneously dispersing in a solution [156].

Carbon based nanomaterials are highly on demand when it comes to any environmental research as it has rare hybridization properties which allows custom modifications [157]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a fascinating class of carbon nanoparticles with sizes around 10 nm. Toxic metal-based quantum dots can be replaced easily by CQDs as they are more environmentally friendly. Besides, CQD has been employed in several fields like bioimaging, medical diagnosis, biosensing, chemical sensing, photocatalysis, and photovoltaic devices [158].

CQDs have been recently employed in wastewater treatment as these nanomaterials have high stability, effective biocompatibility, and water dispersibility. Along with that CQDs are known to have low fabrication cost and these materials possess photo-stability for higher performance [159] . This advanced class of carbon-based nanomaterials have been researched and modified in past decade for its fascinating optical properties, eco-friendly qualities and for having fewer toxic impacts on environment. Additionally, these materials are highly hydrophilic [159]. Unlike GO sheets, CQDs can be voluntarily integrated into different membrane for RO, NF, and PRO process because of their small size of the particles with highly functional groups which can easily form chemical bonds with different compounds [160]. Modification can be carried out on the dense-selective layer or the substrate layer and even on the entire membrane by CQDs making it very functional for different applications. Material cost can be minimized by just utilizing the dense-selective layer of the membrane which has CQDs. Moreover, the limitations like weak mechanical properties and hydrophobicity of polymeric substances such as polysulfone, polyethersulfone, polyvinylidene (PVDF), and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) can be mitigated by the new class of CQD nanofillers [160].

Incorporation of CQDs in membrane modification for PRO process was observed in a study by Gai et al. Two types of membranes were used for comparative analysis of CQDs functionalities, these were TFC-PES and the other one was TFC-PES-PDA-CQD which had the surface layer covered by CQD nanoparticles [161]. As the thickness of CQD layer is about 3 nm, it will only form a one layer on the surface of PDA and will not cover much space. In this experiment, feed solution was high alginate concentration (1 g/L) and draw solution of 0.81 mol/L NaCl was used [162,163]. A power density of 11 W/m2 at 15 bar pressure was achieved for TFC-PES-PDA-CQD membrane. On the other hand, TFC-PES generated only 8.8 W/m2 .Therefore, a remarkable increase in surface hydrophilicity of the substrate was observed after the modification of membrane by CQDs [164]. Moreover, for uncoated PES material the water contact angle is 85.5 ± 3.1°, whereas it significantly reduced to 45.5 ± 3.2° for PES-PDA-CQD. The improvement in surface hydrophilicity would assist to achieve high water flux [162]. Besides, bacterial diffusion test was also conducted on these membranes. The results showed that CQD modified membranes implied in PRO process can enhance antibacterial activity and produce antibiofouling tendency [165]. In addition to that, CQDs integrated in the selective PA layer of PRO hollow fiber TFN membranes by presented a power density of almost 34.2 W/m2 [161]. These data represent CQDs great potentials as nanomaterial used for different membrane modification and its wide application for osmotic power generation worldwide.

Considering the above stated studies, it is clear that the nanomaterials have added more interest in nanotechnology by providing membrane fabrication with increased membrane efficiency and performance [166]. The application of nanomaterials has displayed the desirable outcomes needed in proficient membrane technology like enhanced pure water flux and permeability, hydrophilicity, mechanical and thermal stabilities. The incorporation of nanomaterials has provided long-term resistance to membrane and also helped the membrane to achieve both anti-bacterial and anti-fouling properties along with salt rejection properties for both FO and PRO systems [167,166]. According to Table 2 it can be noted that among all the nanomaterials mentioned in this paper HNTs and GO based TFN membrane showed highest power density of 16.7 W/m2 and water permeability of 2.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The membrane also displayed high mechanical strength and improved functionalities. Similarly another TFN based membrane having PA active layer modified with SiO2 nanomaterial showed a power density of 15.20 W/m2 and had water flux of 36.50 Lm−2 h−1 which was not observed in any other nanomaterial-based membrane. However, even with nanomaterials the membranes might face difficulties like fouling problem along with cost and operational tradeoffs for long term functionality [168]. To solve this issue, recent hybrid nanocomposites are added to different nanomaterials for giving advanced qualities like elevated surface area, high adsorption kinetics, enhanced mechanical and physical steadiness [167].

Nanosized fibers used in membrane modification are known as nanofiber supported membranes. Although nanofibers are relatively new and have fabrication complications, but it has great potentials to support material for different TCF and TFN membranes used in osmotic processes [57]. Recently, many methods of nanofiber preparation have been developed namely self-assembly, electrospinning, template synthesis, and phase separation method. Additionally, a variety of polymers such as keratin, collagen, silk fibroin, cellulose, gelatin, polylactic acid (PLA), polylactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA), polyethylene-co-vinylacetate (PEVA), and polysaccharides have been implied in production of nanofiber based membranes [169]. Many researchers consider nanofibers as the future of membrane technology as it provides high surface to volume ratio, is lightweight, has small diameter and manageable pore structures. Out of the different nanofiber production technologies, the simplest and easy fabrication method is considered to be electrospinning method as it can effortlessly fabricate nonwoven nanofibers, where high-molecular-weight polymers were employed [170]. Structural changes that are done on membrane to reduce ICP should not reduce membrane's ability to withstand technical pressure and must offer a strong upper layer for interfacial polymerization to take place on selective active layer. Electrospinning method is best known for offering nanofibers for support layers to possibly conquer the shortcomings of conventional membrane fabrication methods in accomplishing these difficult modelling standards. In the following section, different polymers, used for nanofiber membrane preparation used in PRO process, have been detailed.

Polyetherimide (PEI) is known to have great chemical, thermal and mechanical stability as it is made of amorphous polymer. PEI also has strong resistance against harsh chemicals like H2O2 and CO2 to maintain its stability and integrity [169]. Several research has been conducted over decades to incorporate PEI based membranes in PRO application to enhance performance and overcome complications. In a study by Li et al., TFC-PEI PRO flat sheet membrane was engineered by phase inversion and IP methods. N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 499.5) was used as the solvent for dope preparation. Moreover, to examine the correlation between the structure of the membrane and PRO performance a membrane characterization test was performed. Besides, pressure cycle experiments and stability tests were performed on the fabricated membrane for examining its mechanical stability. Three types of membrane were synthesized from this experiment namely PEI-1#, PEI-2# and PEI-3# [171]. 13 % ethanol was used to fabricate all three membranes. Whereas PEI-1# had 12 % PEI and 75 % NMP, PEI-2# had 14.5 % PEI and 72.5 % NMP and lastly PEI-3# had 17 % PEI and 70 % NMP respectively. Additionally, the results achieved from this experiment showed that superior power density of 20.9 W/m2 at 15 bar was achieved. Out of all three membranes, TFC PEI-2# membrane had maximum potentials to generate a stable power density of 12.8 W/m2 at 17.2 bar, using 1 M NaCl solution as draw solution and 10 mM NaCl solution as feed solution [171].

Another novel membrane was fabricated in a recent study with TFC membrane comprised of a tiered PEI. This membrane was strengthened by f-CNTs and PA based ultrathin active layer was employed [121]. The potentiality of this membrane was tested by 10-h experiment to check if the membrane can maintain a stable power density for long-term operation. Fig. 10 demonstrates the post treatment images of cross-sectional view of the novel TFC with tiered PEI nanofiber support layer and its properties [53].

Overall, the studies mentioned above have revealed the pertinence of nanofibers usage for developing greater power density PRO membranes. Despite all these modifications, there still remains high probability of membrane distortion still since greater hydraulic pressures is used nanofiber-based PRO membranes. One of the drawbacks of PRO membranes is their inferior mechanical stability. To tackle this issue, a recent TFC-PRO hollow-fiber membrane was manufactured using a finger-like PEI hollow-fiber support. This lead to a maximum power density generation almost 20.9 W/m2 was achieved at 15.1 bar pressure when utilizing 1 M NaCl solution as the draw solution and 1 mM NaCl solution as the feed solution [26]. Moreover, when DI feed solution was used with the same membrane with 1 M NaCl as the draw solution and pressured was increased to 22 bar, a maximum 29.0 W/m2 of power density was achieved in this experiment.

The most largely used materials for production of microfiltration and ultrafiltration membranes for water process applications is polyacrylonitrile based membrane [172]. Currently it has been used in several membrane modification processes for PRO application. PAN is well known for having a porous support for solvent resistant nanofiltration along with its good mechanical and film forming properties. Several attempts and laboratory experiments are conducted worldwide to produce novel membranes incorporated with PAN. A recent study with the same aim developed a membrane with combination of cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) added to PAN nanofiber PRO membrane. This membrane was fabricated with tailor-made flat sheet construction equipment. The hypothesis of this experiment was to check if the addition of CNC with PAN boosted the mechanical strength in the PAN membranes used in PRO applications. The results showed that a greater water flux of 405.38 LMH was obtained when 1 M NaCl was used as draw solution and DI water as feed solution [83]. The membrane orientation of this experiment was made in a way that the draw solution is set against the active layer and the feed solution is located against the support layer. Maintaining this condition, 2 % CNC was supplemented with PAN nanocomposite nanofiber TFC-PRO membranes with the goal to achieve better water flux. This certainly allowed the flux increase in the membranes. Moreover, to compare anther membrane with 5 % CNC additive was tested which resulted in enhanced water flux but the flux value was decreased with more addition of CNC. It was noted that for 60 min of this experiment, the original flux value was maintained by most of the fabricated membranes. Thus, it was concluded that although the addition of CNT in PAN membrane is ideal for the development of PRO membranes, it is important to maintain optimal ratio where higher water flux will be achieved with lowest reverse salt flux [83]. In this research, it can be easily that 1 % CNC added PAN nanocomposite nanofiber TFC membranes provide the best results. on the other hand, 2 % CNC addition to the PAN polymer matrix gives superior water flux and the lowest reversed salt flux. The outcomes from the experiment showed that PAN/CNC nanocomposite TFN membranes offer utmost water flux with least inverted salt flux values. In a study by Lee et al., the team fabricated nanofiber-based PRO membranes using a PAN nanofiber support as nanofibers and are known for having high potential for membrane as they have high porosity. The result from the nanofiber based newly developed membranes attained a power density of 21.3 W/m2 at 15.2 bar pressure using 1.06 M NaCl as draw solution and 8.0 W/m2 was achieved at 11.5 bar pressure when the draw solution concentration was changed to 0.5 M NaCl keeping DI water as feed solution for both [26]. Additionally, a synthetic fiber that has high thermal and mechanical stability was also incorporated with PAN. A multilayer PRO hollow-fiber membrane was developed consisting of PAN and polybenzimidazole (PBI). PBI was used as the outer selectivity layer and PAN was used as a mechanically robust support layer. As PBI is well known for its high hydrophilicity, chemical, thermal, and acidic stability, it was expected to enhanced the functionality of PAN membrane [173,26]. The result from this experiment demonstrated a maximum power density of 6.2 W/m2 at 15 bar when 1 M NaCl was used as draw solution and 10 mM NaCl was used as feed solution [26]. Furthermore, a recent study worked with the most commonly found TFC flat-sheet membranes on PAN support using several post-treatments. The study stated a power density of 2.60 W/m2 at 10 bar pressure employing the draw solution 3.5 wt% NaCl. Incredibly thin PA selective layers of this TFC membranes confirmed greater efficacy in a preponderance of liquid-separation use [56].

To summarize, nanofibers are highly desirable due to their enhanced features like thickness, porosity, hydrophilicity, and polarity. These properties are highly needed for proficient membrane functioning in both FO and PRO systems. Highly diverse and tunable characteristics of nanofibers that includes variations in electrospinning operating parameters along with precursor polymer, solvent, and other application-specific additives makes them adaptable to different applications [174]. From Table 4 , it is observed that the highest power density was achieved by TFC-PEI HF membrane which resulted in 29.0 W/m2 under 22 bar operating pressure with 1.0 M NaCl draw solution and DI feed solution. Similarly PAN with nanofiber support also gave a power density of 21.3 W/m2 under 15.2 bar operating pressure. This suggested that with increased pressure, higher power density was achieved. Therefore, nanofibers can be highly advantageous for PRO application since it provides low fouling tendency, high salt rejection, high water flux, and low reverse salt flux to the membranes used in the process. In the future, this can also be combined FO–MD or FO–RO systems to acquire pure water for drinking purposes which can be encouraging for industrial suitability to regain drinkable water [174].

PRO hybrids are for achieving higher power generation through advanced membrane process. The PRO unit is often combined along with FO and RO units for obtaining better outputs. FO membranes are less energy intrusive and are much closer to commercialization. However, for FO membranes to be used in PRO applications, the membranes must be robust and must have the capacity to withstand high pressure since PRO system uses high bar of pressure [175]. Several theoretical hybrid models were created based on energy consumption for recovering water by various combined processes that includes RO-PRO, RO-MD and RO-MD-PRO [176]. The most commonly used ones are RO-PRO, PRO-FO and PRO-thermal which have been discussed in this section since these systems are boosted with additional features from industrial wastewater streams. The proposed systems are best known as optimal designs that can lower the specific energy consumption of membrane-based treatment systems specially in desalination and water technology sector [177].

The effect of temperature or thermal energy is very prevalent in the performance of PRO process as any temperature variation between the feed solution and draw solution can create a temperature gradient across heterogeneous-nature PRO membrane as shown in Fig. 11 . This can result in heat transport around the surface or even inside the membrane. When the temperature raises in feed or draw solution, both water permeability and solute absorbency of the PRO membrane can increase. An increase in these two parameters can lead to higher draw solution temperature [178]. However, the diffusivity and mass transfer coefficient are observed to increase along with increasing temperature.

On the other hand, change in temperature can affect PRO membrane structure parameter as dilation of polymer takes place as thermal effect. This ultimately causes the PRO membrane to become thin and torsion might appear on membrane. However, the softened thin PRO membrane can reach to gain lesser structure parameter, which aids to attain reduced ICP [179]. Besides, increased water flux and improved energy production can be achieved by increasing temperature. As showed in Table 5 , improving feed or draw solution or both solution temperatures in a PRO process promoted high power densities. It was seen that not much energy production progress was made at a low pressure on the draw solution, whereas considerable energy production improvement was noted by increasing applied pressure which indicated that the concurrent effect of the temperature and pressure is crucial for energy production [178]. However, another study by Adhikary et al., have showed that increasing temperature causes slight impact on the structure parameter by increasing it slightly. Working condition of PRO membrane and different operation situations might be linked with such dissimilar observations [180]. Many recent studies were projected towards the evaluation of traditional (single stage) and dual stage CLPRO for power generation from a salinity gradient resource. The results demonstrated that the dual stage CLPRO process surpassed the performance of conventional CLPRO process. CLPRO also increased the power generation by 18 % by the addition of it in a second stage of PRO membrane. Moreover, for the regeneration of the draw solution, multi-effect distillation (MED) was selected by benefiting from an available source of waste heat energy. In this process, a draw solution of TDS with 35 g/L was used against the feed solution of DI water. Experimentally a power generation of 2.9 W/m2 was observed and theoretically 2.8 W/m2 was obtained. On the other hand, with 60 g/L draw solution a power generation of 8.3 W/m2 was observed and theoretically 8 W/m2 was found [181]. On the other hand, for distillation in the middle easter countries, the MSF distillation is frequently utilized as it runs on evaporation and flash principle. In this process, seawater is typically fed into the tubes of the heat exchangers to condense the produced steam at each flash stage. MSF contains up to 21 % of portion as desalination capacity. Whereas MED used in desalination uses successive stages at decreasing levels of pressure and temperature.

PRO hybridized processes are quickly evolving, and they are considered very favorable process type for energy efficiency and cost reduction. In particular, an RO-PRO hybridized process turns out to be the most popular since RO marks a huge role in reducing water stresses globally. As RO is known to be an energy exhaustive process, the hybridization of RO-PRO is widely studied among researchers. Although the implementation of PRO process in combination with RO is believed to be an ideal approach, its process is far from maturation [182]. Previously many researchers tried to merge a PRO process with traditional thermal desalination procedures (e.g., multi-effect distillation). Numerical analysis approach was conducted to test the feasibility of the hybridized processes. However, a novel approach was taken to expand the scale of a PRO-hybridized process such as RO-membrane distillation-PRO. In recent days, most research papers are related to PRO-hybridized processes are devoted to the RO-PRO process. Therefore, comprehensive insight about RO-PRO process is considered for several hybridized processes [182]. As the output of RO desalination unit comprises of two streams, one which is a very low-salinity potable water stream and the other one is highly saline brine stream which is higher than the input. The combination process of RO and PRO can be made by pretreating the brackish water from river by RO unit and using the resulting permeate as the feed solution of PRO unit. This can be a very potential model for places where the river-water properties such as salinity and osmotic pressure vary seasonally. Lastly, the hypersaline brine rejected from RO unit can be utilized as the PRO unit's draw solution. A study by Achilli et al. has shown how the high pressure of the brine can be transferred through the traditional RO-PX arrangement which is done by pressure exchanger to push it into the RO feed solution. RO feed solution used simulated seawater for all the configurations and along with RO-PRO. On the other hand, 1-μm filtered municipal tape water was used for the PRO feed solution. RO had operating hydraulic pressure ranging from 3500 and 4800 kPa to achieve required freshwater recoveries. A needle valve was used to form the pressure that reduced the pressure of the brine stream leaving the RO module towards the programmed pressure of the PRO module. Besides some energy was lost to the surrounds from the pressure as heat. The pressure lasted from 100 and 240 kPa in PRO feed solution. This range was lower than the draw solution range which was 700 and 1700 kPa. Therefore, no damage was caused to the membrane [183]. The plant was examined using the brine of RO with a power density range of 1.1–2.3 W/m2 gain an average power densities by experimental methods [53,183]. In a recent study, to analyze the impact of driving force on flux, a feed solution of deionized water was used against three different draw solution concentration of 30, 200 and 350 g/L at 20 bars of hydraulic pressure [184]. The results proved that best power density can be achieved high concentrations of 350 g/L i.e. of 16 W/m2 which is adequate to run an RO system as it requires power >5 W/m2. Future studies could examine the numerous feed and draw solutions to enhance the PRO process [184]. The most common process for treating highly saline feed solution is using two-pass RO desalination process since it offers a low-salinity permeate solution. A study by Al-Zainati et al. examined the importance of the energy produced by the dual-stage PRO from the RO brine stream. PRO, RO, and energy recovery device along with their models were determined as the main components of the DSPRO-RO hybrid system. For solving the hybrid system model, software named Dymola and Modelica modelling language was used. It had two distinct flowsheets were the first consisted of a two-pass RO, while the second was a hybrid of a two-pass RO (2RO)-DSPRO system. RO feed solution had seawater salinities of 40 and 45 g/L. The feed solution of DSPRO process was 1 g/L tertiary treated wastewater. The calculation of net specific energy consumption for 40 and 45 g/L salinities of 2RO and 2RO-DSPRO systems respectively was made. It was found that, the 2RO-DSPRO system was 14.7 % more energy efficient than the 2RO system at 47 % recovery rate. The energy saving rate for 45 g/L seawater salinity was 17.5 % at a recovery rate of 47 %. The energy obtained by desalination was between 3.25 and 3.49 kWh/m3 for the 2RO system. On the other hand, for the 2RO-DSPRO system it was between 2.91 and 2.97 kWh/m3. The findings proved an excellent potential of integrating the 2RO with the DSPRO for decreasing desalination's energy consumption and for eliminating the environmental impacts [185].

Furthermore, brine management can be conducted successfully by the model presented for incorporating PRO with the RO. This is done by feeding the brine back into PRO units to generate a salinity gradient for water transport via membrane and produce energy. This model is considered ideal for grid-tied offshore wind power [186].

The process of PRO is found to be more relatable to FO than RO and therefore the membranes used in FO are more agreeable for application in PRO. As mentioned earlier, PRO technology is severely restricted by fouling of membrane if a real waste effluent is used as the feed solution. To overcome this issue the hybrid of PRO-FO can be very effective as forward osmosis can be used as a pretreatment means to isolate water from the wastewater stream to the inter-loop solution. Consequently, the diluted inter-loop solution could serve as a clean feed to the PRO unit with significantly less fouling tendency. With the advantages obtained from FO like low fouling proclivity, simple membrane washing, and minimum external energy requirement, the recently created FO-PRO hybrid system can effortlessly withstand power generation [96]. A novel FO-PRO hybrid system was created for sustainable osmotic power generation. As real waste effluents collected from water recycling plant went through FO system before being served as feed solution for PRO, the performance of PRO membrane was drastically increased due to less fouling tendency. Besides, a water flux of 10–15 LHM was generated by FO with superior fouling resistance. The PRO unit in this set up generated >5 W/m2 power density with 50 % diluted seawater desalination brine (SWBr) [96]. The main characteristics of membrane used in PRO-FO hybrid must have high water permeability, low salt reverse flux and minimal internal concentration polarization [53]. Since cellulose acetate possess all the characteristics mentioned, they are used as best membranes for PRO application.

There are several studies based on various FO membranes for PRO, among which the commonly investigated is commercial FO asymmetric CTA based flat sheet membrane. From the results of a study related to CTA, by the utilization of deionized water (DI) as feed and 35 g/L NaCl solution achieved a maximum power density of 2.73 W/m2 at 9.72 bar pressure. Moreover, by increasing the NaCl concentration to 60 g/L, deionized water as feed, and under same pressure a power density of 5.06 W/m2 was also obtained [187]. Furthermore, a peak power density of 4.5 W/m2 was also obtained by the utilization of cellulose triacetate FO membrane in PRO process when 1 M and 10 mM NaCl were used as draw and feed solution respectively [75]. From this experiment, it was determined that the PRO mode of FO operation uses high initial water fluxes when CTA membranes are used. Further observations from different studies related to the characteristics of the surface of the support layer in terms of surface roughness and loose-fitted pores were also analyzed. Overall to reach the standards created by Statkraft which is 5 W/m2, the salinity of the inter-loop solution of PRO must be 0.1 M, to generate density >5 W/m2 [95]. However, FO membranes possesses poor mechanical strength compared to PRO membranes as no pressure is applied in FO applications. Thus, FO membranes properties can lead to membrane damages in at the high operation pressure of PRO process [95] . However, further analysis to operate this hybrid FO/PRO/RO system with equal FO and RO membrane area were also investigated in many PRO hybrid studies. Results from those studies demonstrated that at 0.163 M interloop concentration (feed solution), the system is modelled to produce 7.15 W/m2 while requiring a net SEC of 0.76 kW electricity for every 1 m3/h clean water produced [7].

To summarize, among all the PRO-thermal hybrids mentioned in Table 5 the highest attainable power density ranged from 16.55 to 29.48 W/m2 where draw solution of NaCl (1.2 M) was used against feed solution of 10 mM NaCl. On the other hand, in between PRO-MED and PRO-MSF, the highest power density was attained by PRO-MSF combination that resulted in high power generation that ranged from 2.5 to 5 (kWh/m3); where fresh water was used as draw solution and sea water as feed solution respectively. From Table 6 PRO-RO hybrid gave the maximum achievable power density of 16 W/m2, which is way higher than other hybrids. This suggests that PRO combined with RO is the best form of hybrid process which can help in attaining highest outcome. The power generation is mainly provided by PRO unit along with the reduction of brine discharge when a hybrid of RO-PRO is present. Different studies suggest to keep the water recovery rate >29 % to increase power generation along with increasing energy consumption [176]. Moreover, when the novel PRO-RO is compared to previous FO-RO models, it is observed that PRO-RO needs greater area for FO while RO-PRO requires lower membrane area. But both the models are much more expensive than previous FO-RO design since FO membranes for this type of model can be found at lower cost. However, coupling PRO with FO and adding RO membrane process to it can lower the cost of desalination plant and can be an ideal model for optimal power generation [188].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance limiting factors for PRO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Although PRO membrane is a fast-growing technology for efficient and sustainable power generation, it is also prone to many restraining factors that slows down PRO performance. Among multiple factors that affects the efficiency of PRO membranes the four main performance limiting factors includes concentration polarization, reverse solute diffusion, membrane fouling and mechanical stability [12]. These membranes often endure some critical disruptions that strictly prevents the possibility of wide practical applications of PRO membranes and hampers their commercialization ability.

Concentration polarization takes place when there is an increase or decrease in the concentration of a particular element at the periphery layer close to the surface of the membrane because of the selective transport through the membrane [189]. A concentration profile is generated as the solute is retained by the membrane, especially in processes driven by pressure like microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, or reverse osmosis. Pressure retarded osmosis process is not different from them and it is also vulnerable to this important parameter that affects the proficiency of PRO membranes. As in PRO process, selective transport of solutes occurs through a membrane, the feed side of the membrane surface retains the solute that is highly concentrated and permeate side faces the diluted solution. As a result, the accumulation of solutes occurs mainly near the membrane surface and this becomes a main barrier for large-scale PRO functions [190]. Concentration polarization can be of two types depending on membranes that are driven by osmotic pressure. Internal concentration polarization happens within the membrane substrate and external concentration polarization (ECP) appears in the feed and draw solutions, which further can be classified as concentrative ECP and dilutive ECP, respectively. Both ICP and ECP drastically compromises the osmotic driving force across the membrane active layer, causing in water flux reduction and shortage of power density [12]. The issue of ICP is more prominent when a nonideal composite membrane is used in a standard PRO process as the salt gradient causes concentrative ICP and decrease the osmotic force driving the water across the membranes. There remains a high chance of ICP to occur when thin film is supported by a porous substrate. Membranes with high water infiltration and greater salt rejection are vital for high PRO performance. Besides, ECP occurs when the support layer of the membrane sides with the feed solution and solute is conveyed by the water flow from the bulk solution to the surface of the active layer. As a result, the feed solutes get accumulated at the surface of the active layer and increases growth in the feed concentration [191].

A study was conducted by Fei et al., on TFC membrane running in PRO mode and detected that the membrane experienced a severe damage of integrity and selectivity after gypsum scaling in PRO process. This occurred due to the scaling which resulted in having lower limiting flux mainly due to high structural parameter (S) that induced ICP [46]. Two main parameters like membrane productivity (water flux) and selectivity (salt rejection) are highly affected by ICP as it weakens membrane performance regardless of the process. The issue of ICP is different in FO when compared to PRO because the direction of solute and water fluxes are opposite. Although it is not feasible to eradicate the impacts of ICP in FO and PRO processes, but it is possible to fairly decrease its adverse effects by enhancing the membrane structure [192]. Selection of thin membranes that are not bounded to a fabric support layer is important in PRO process for allowing higher water flux. Results from a study based on FO demonstrated less susceptibility to concentration polarization as thinner support layer compared to PRO membranes were used in FO process. Thus, thinner membranes are used more often in PRO studies [190].

A special transportation phenomenon in osmotically driven membrane process where solutes like Na+, Mg2+ and Ca2+ continuously diffuses to the feed side as water with less concentration flows from the feed to draw solution is called reverse solute diffusion. Along with the concentration salinity gradient, hydraulic pressure applied can also develop the RSD in PRO process which ultimately results in distortion of membranes structure. Many studies have demonstrated the capabilities of organic fouling and gypsum scaling on the support layer resulted from the influence of RSD [193]. Besides, loss of draw solution is observed in reverse solute diffusion as water transports from draw solution into the feed which ultimately results in extra expenditure for maintaining and renewal of the draw solution. Additionally, feed solution composition is heavily altered because of the draw solutes build up in the feed. This impacts the movement of active sludge in osmotic membrane bioreactor which ultimately causes membrane fouling [179]. Moreover, feed solutions are known to have microorganism which can cause biofouling, the main obstacle in PRO. In this situation, RSD from the draw solution may modify biofouling inclination. A recent study has discovered the relationship between RSD and biofouling in PRO by investigating the impacts of applied hydraulic pressure (ΔP) and types of draw solution on RSD characteristics on the support layer. The results from this experiment showed that RSD of Na+ displayed insignificant consequences on biofouling with different ΔP values. On the other hand, severe biofouling in PRO process was caused by RSD of Ca2+ due to declination of water flux. Furthermore, insignificant impacts were observed on biofouling in PRO due to the minor effects of Mg2+ on biofilm formation in Fig. 12 .

Membrane fouling is easily described as the buildup of undesirable deposits on the outside of the membrane or inside the pores of the membrane. One of the major barriers in membrane-based hybrid processes for water treatment for energy production is membrane fouling. Membrane fouling is known to reduce permeate flux, drop permeate condition, raise operational cost, and also shorten membrane life [56]. Membrane fouling has the potentials to alter membrane features and morphology significantly which can change the hydrophilicity of the membrane and cause a substantial drop in permeate quality [8]. In addition to that, membrane fouling can also be triggered by intricate physical, chemical, and even biological interactions between the foulants including organic or even inorganic. This decreases permeate flux can expand the operation cost of membranes. Therefore, to successfully diminish membrane fouling and enhance fouling reversibility within the porous substrate layer continues to be a major burden for making PRO marketable [192].

Several investigations have been performed to overcome this problem of membrane fouling and many successful results were obtained. Out of which, an experiment was conducted by Han et al., using laboratory-scale crossflow PRO system for examining PRO membrane fouling. Here, different concentration of NaCl solutions were used as draw solution and DI water was used as feed solution for gaining the standards for PRO performance of the membranes. Later on to check the effectiveness of the membrane's antifouling capacity, actual wastewater brine was gathered from wastewater recycling plant and artificial solutions that included single foulants were used as the feed. The special membrane used in this procedure was TFC-PES/Torlon and TFC-PES/Torlon-P[MPC-co-AEMA] membranes [53,194]. The results demonstrated exceptional fouling resistance towards both organic foulants and inorganic scaling. The main reasons behind this superior antifouling property are firstly because of a strong hydration layer produced on top of P[MPC-co-AEMA] coating layer due to the excellent hydrophilicity of the zwitterionic brush. Secondly, the electrostatic interactions between the membrane surface and the foulants in the feed was decreased by the net electrical neutrality of the zwitterions. And lastly, inorganic scaling was repressed by the phosphonate functional groups of the zwitterions that acted as powerful chelating agents to form stable complexes with free Ca2+ ions [194] Another study was conducted by Wan et al., using AlCl3 which is an acid coagulant and NaAlO2 which is caustic coagulant to remove phosphate and maintain a mild pH environment most effectively. For the fouling studies in PRO mode, 1000 mL of AlCl3 and NaAlO2 pretreated and untreated wastewater concentrates were used as the feed solutions. The untreated wastewater was saturated with Ca3(PO4)2 significantly reduced flux by 45.2 % in the beginning of the test and further reduction of 30.7 % was observed with proceeding time. A total 25 % recovery of wastewater concentration was seen. Moreover, the coagulation pretreatments improved the water flux power by three times and a power density of 16.3 LMH and 9.1 W/m2 was achieved when a permeation of 250 mL volume was used [195]. This study confirmed that both acidic and caustic aluminum coagulants can successfully eliminate phosphate from the wastewater concentrate and thus prevent the membrane fouling issue from PRO process and other similar desalination systems [195].

Fouling of membranes can be caused by both inorganic and organic substances. Scientists have been constantly researching on ways to overcome the issue and to investigate the mitigation of both organic and inorganic fouling in PRO by osmotically driven cleaning methods. A lab scale study was performed by Sim et al. on-fouling issue of PRO membranes that focused on efficient cleaning procedures that included: (i) Osmotic Backwashing (OB), (ii) Reverse Osmosis Flushing (ROF), and (iii) Pressure Assisted Osmotic Backwashing (PAOB). A comparative evaluation was conducted focusing on these three osmotically driven (OM) cleaning methods and the recovery performance of all the membranes were observed. The water flux (Jw) was the comparison factor for all three membranes. The direction of membrane was AL-DS position where the draw solution was kept in contact with active layer of the membrane of PRO. The membrane that was used for this evaluation was a flat sheet membrane made of stainless steel with an area of 0.064 m2 [196]. The membrane also consisted of a support layer which was thicker than the active layer so that it can resist applied pressure from draw side during the operation. The 3-version PRO membrane was obtained from a Toray (Toray Co., Korea). The first version of the membrane was simply modified with humic acid (HA). The second one was modified with humic acid and CaSO4 and the 3rd version of the membrane consisted of humic acid and CaCO3. After all three membranes were run under PRO mode and a flux vs Time diagram was obtained in Fig. 13 . The condition for these membranes were AL-DS(PRO mode) and pressure applied was 15 bar at 20⸰C. the flow rate of both feed and draw solution was kept at 1 LPM. The results obtained demonstrated a declination of permeate flux by three types of membranes containing three different substances. The flux declination was varied depending on the fouling matters. After 1 h, sever flux reduction was noted. The results showed that the organic-inorganic substance (humic acid + CaCO3) which is the third membrane had more impact on the membrane compared to the others. The fouling substances determined the fouling tendency of the membranes. Furthermore, when the cleaning method were applied, both ROF and PAOB utilized pressure during the washing procedure. Both methods induced membrane deformation due to high pressure. The PRO performance was negatively affected as deformed membranes were present for a long operation period. The recovery rate of all three types of cleaning methods applied the fouled membrane was similar at initial operation. However, the PRO membrane performance was getting different depending on the cleaning methods. 92 % of recovery rate was achieved by PAOB. PAOB was more effective method to rinse membrane compared to ROF and OB [196].

With proceeding time, different ongoing research gave rise to other techniques that required the use of chemicals for modifying membranes. A study was conducted by hollow fiber TFC PRO membrane which was chemically modified and was attached to hyperbranched polyglycerol (HPG) onto a PES hollow fiber substrate before IP method. The modified membrane generated a power density of 6.7 W/m2 and also showed antifouling capabilities at 16 bar when 3.5 % NaCl was used as draw solution and DI water as feed [162]. Moreover, another membrane that consisted of Sulfonate hyperbranched polyglycerol grafting of aminosilane, specifically 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) was another potential membrane that demonstrated improved fouling resistance of the membrane. For testing this membrane, municipal wastewater was used as the feed solution for more practical application [162]. Additionally, another chemically modified membrane that consisted of zwitterionic 2-methacryloyloxyethylphosphorylcholine (MPC) which displayed 7.7 W m power density at 15 bar. The membrane had improved antifouling capacity. Fouling in membrane can be a major obstacle in the efficacy of PRO performance. Besides, antifouling membranes alone cannot resist fouling, pre-treatment is required for better improvement of the whole process. When using wastewater concentrate as feed solution, fewer contamination tendency can make PRO process more functional. Combining PRO process with other desalination technologies demonstrated more benefits over standalone PRO setup. This was because when the diluted feed solution is provided to PRO process, the chances of fouling decreases because of low salinity and foulants in feed solution. Over years, conventional membrane technologies are widely used which must be replaced soon with advanced technologies that utilizes pretreatment processes that gives better efficiency of PRO process than the classical methods [162].

The main purpose of PRO process is to have greater efficiency for power generation and achieving high power density compared to other salinity energies [98]. The base load power generated by PRO process is highly reliable and more economical than any other renewable energy sources [197,198,32,199,11]. Unfortunately, the conclusive year for PRO studies was in 2016 and the research curve has been stagnant since then. Therefore, the process of commercializing PRO remained very uncertain [25]. Lack of large-scale osmotic power plants makes it difficult to estimate the current costs and material price for PRO process. However, a recent study revealed a net power density of 56.4 W/m2 is needed for a PRO system to reach a $0.074/kWh, but maximum research only demonstrated power density of only 5 W/m2 which is tenfold smaller than the required criteria for economic practicality [200]. Besides, the capital cost of PRO process is higher compared to other expenses. On top of that, if the membrane has low power density, then capital cost tends to increase. For example, the capital cost of power density of 1 W/m2 is about $20,000 per kW [200]. On the other hand, the power density of 5 W/m2 would be $4000 per kW [200]. Moreover, extra cost is required for the installed membranes that needs to be changed after a specific lifespan. Apart from membrane maintenance, a large capital cost is needed for other facilities like pre-treatment, pumps, pressure exchangers, hydro turbines, and other devices. However, the capital cost could be reduced by selecting an appropriate location to lunch an osmotic plant as it can reduce the additional expense required for intake and outfall systems [201].

The viability of pre-treatment system requires extensive efforts to reduce biggest energy losses as reduction of pre-treatment from 0.22 to 0.16 MJ/m3 which is around 25 % declination. This as a result can produce up to 50 % more power than the original [201]. The installation cost also makes a difference as the hybrid systems are usually lower in cost than the stand-alone PRO plants. These are some significant variables to study the PRO economic viability and verify the approximate profits of a PRO facility. Another concern of PRO is the power density generation which can be modified by using osmotically engineered proficient membranes which has the ability to permeate water flux higher than 50 L/m2h while still being selective with solute rejection >99 %. Furthermore, the membrane should have the ability to tolerate hydraulic pressures higher than 5.5 MPa (800 psi) [202]. Therefore, conducting efficient economical calculations, having knowledge about power generation, and knowing the selling price of electricity grid are mandatory for trading PRO process at a profitable rate. Different studies are focusing on the prospect for PRO scale up. One study by Manzoor et al., demonstrated the development of a tool that can assess the scalability of PRO membranes by incorporating a comprehensive mass transfer model for the membrane unit. This stimulator considers the effects of concentration polarization on membrane and can also function against bench-scale experimental data at various experimental conditions [203]. Another approach for PRO scale up was conducted by maximum power point tracking technique that guaranteed highest extraction of power for the duration of instant salinity variation. This technique was formed mainly on whale optimization method that used differential evolution algorithm which helped to conquer the problems related to reduced tracking proficiency and constant state oscillation that are usually observed in traditional methods [204]. The scaled up PRO system elevated the efficiency and robustness of the system which surpassed widely used systems [204] Moreover, membrane module can highly influence membrane fouling impacts if suitable modules are not used in practical and large-scale application of PRO. A recent study demonstrated effective power generation of 8.9 W/m2 at 15 bars with PRO modules having an effective area of 0.5m2. this module was found to scale up the performance criteria for TFC hollow fiber membrane in PRO process [205].

Pilot and full scale demonstration viability of PRO is one of the major challenges in current time [25]. Hence, several studies focusing on PRO complications like effect of fouling, ways of improving of membrane modules, effective modelling and process optimization methods has been carried out. The improvements in transportation properties related PRO membranes that deals with water permeability coefficient (A), the solute permeability coefficient (B), and the structural parameter (S) are the main factors that affects the full scale implementation of PRO process [206]. To have better forecast of practical performances, the need of further research is required as only few findings have addressed about issues related to membrane characteristics, fouling and spacer designs. Therefore, two main structures were marketed for the advancement of full-scale modules, spiral wound membrane modules (applied to flat-sheet membranes) and for hollow fiber membrane modules based on operating requirements [76]. Considering the efficiency of different components of PRO process, another study by G. O'Toole et al., revealed the net energy creation from a full-scale PRO model system. The newly modelled PRO had PV with one PRO spiral wound membrane module disposed in series and in parallel were considered. After optimizing the power gain, value of PD was 3.25 W m − 2 and a permeate specific energy generation of 0.42 kWh m − 3 was obtained considering PVs in series [207]. Another study focused on the optimization of full-scale HF PRO module (5 in.) for increasing the energy withdrawal was conducted by Y. Chen et al. besides, more modules were highlighted in the series that showed the link between lower power density but with higher the generated energy [208].

For generating higher energy, PRO processes are applied in either direct energy generation or in a hybrid process. Besides, several full-scale PRO modules need to be organized in series for obtaining the preferred Qp [209]. Additionally full-scale RO modules have few marginal requirements for maximizing Q D, out and Q F, in and minimum Q F, out which are usually set by the producers by considering full-scale PRO systems under operation or under simulation. Therefore, the weakest points of the technology can be identified when these boundary conditions are taken into consideration and more realistic results can be obtained from it [209]. However, actual energy production amount cannot be calculated from lab scale PRO test results, where the actual salinity gradient decreases. Only the net energy from a full-scale PRO process should be wisely analyzed with respect to reduced salinity gradient, ineffective hydraulic devices, and loss of pressure alongside the channel. A net energy production (NEP) analysis must be based on pressure loss along the channel for viscous dissipation since it plays an essential role to estimate the energy expenditure in a full-scale PRO process. In recent years, full scale PRO models have been created by researchers based on changing parameters like pressure loss and inefficient hydraulic devices along the channel length in order to study these thoroughly [210,211,212]. This work focused on NEP analysis that maintained the fundamental modelling method in order to forecast the full-scale performance of a PRO process. Additionally, accounts were taken for turbine energy production and the energy consumption by pumps (minus effect) along with ERD energy saving methods for effectiveness of hydraulic devices. Nevertheless, net positive energy cannot be obtained from full scale PRO system all the time if proper design option is not selected. Therefore, hydraulic parameters like system capacity, membrane area, module length, the DS and FS flow rates, and the mechanical pressure on the DS side to produce NEP have been studied by many researchers [93,212].

Moreover, some papers suggests that the pressure loss and inefficiencies of hydraulic devices can often result in inadequate extraction of net positive energy from the salinity gradient between seawater and river water. Therefore, another study carried out by Kim et al., explained the functional limit of energy production from seawater by full-scale PRO process where the effect of system's capability on NEP of full-scale PRO was gradually investigated by creating module-scale PRO model [210]. Furthermore, a simulation tool was provided by a study conducted by Ruiz-García et al., for full-scale PRO systems that lets precise values of PRO-related energy generation to be made that helps the user to approximate the performance and SOWs of full-scale systems [213]. Besides, the introductory module features such as permeability coefficients, active area, porosity, etc. can also be evaluated by the user in this model. Thus, other membrane features like permeability coefficients as well as the energy balance that includes pumps and ERD performance must be analyzed in future studies. This will allow proper identification of limiting factors and avoid such issues in the production of potential models with full-scale PRO plants [213].

After performance estimation of different PRO membranes on lab scale to clarify energy recovery, few pilot scale research were carried out for investigating prime design and operating factors of a PRO system. Based on this, the RO-PRO hybrid plant was constructed in Japan and another pilot plant of RO-membrane distillation-PRO hybrid pilot plant was built in South Korea [214,215]. The first pilot PRO power generation plant that was created by Statkraft in Norway in the year 2009 took the advantage of the salinity gradient between sea and river water. Besides, research on osmotic power generation was also conducted in Fukuoka, Japan. A pilot plant with hollow fiber PRO membrane modules was constructed in seawater desalination procedure by gathering the osmotic energy of seawater brine in the year 2012. However, only few of these pilot scale studies were presented since substantial improvements has taken place from lab-scale to pilot-scale demonstrations in PRO systems. Nevertheless, these studies regarded PRO as the most feasible method to yield salinity gradient energy.

Additionally, countable studies have tested the performance of PRO simulation by utilizing pilot-scale PRO hollow-fiber modules under different operational situations [28,216–220]. In the study performed by Kakihana, Y et al., an investigation was conducted based on different PRO operating conditions to test two different types of pilot scale membrane modules consisting of HF and SW configurations. The operating conditions consisted of DS concentrations, the flow rates of DS and FS, and applied hydraulic pressure differences to check various factors. This investigation between two modules of PRO performance supplied valuable perceptions for understanding the design of large-scale PRO plants. Moreover, the water permeability of polyamide spiral-wound was confirmed to be seven times greater in comparison to cellulose triacetate hollow-fiber module. Additionally, 13 times higher salt permeability was achieved using spiral-wound polyamide compared to cellulose triacetate hollow-fiber module. Therefore, it was proved that hollow-fiber membrane has less DS leakage when compared to polyamide spiral-wound [221].

Besides, another study by Chung, K et al., demonstrated the SWRO-PRO hybrid performance in power generation by investigating it by pilot scale system by evaluating its key design parameters and different operating conditions. Another semi-pilot scale PRO system composed of TFC PRO spiral wound membrane that were of 4-in. each was run for comparative study. Better results were obtained by semi-pilot scale study as it had higher energy recovery rate with increasing salinity of draw solution. This result was obtained due to the enhanced osmotic pressure which resulted in tougher driving force throughout the PRO membrane. Several important factors like optimal permeate flux, feed solution recovery, and PD in the pilot plant were easily determined with the help of this study [222].

Sometimes, feed solution in PRO consists of wastewater which is widely observed in RO-PRO hybrid. This situation causes high fouling in membranes which significantly weakens energy production. In addition to that, fouling can affect membrane performance and reduce its lifetime. In a recent PRO study, the impact of using feedwater and its properties along with several pretreatment methods on flux and power density was examined in bench-scale and pilot-scale systems. The results showed that the fouling behavior in pilot scale PRO system was similar to that of bench scale PRO setup only until primary period of the operation. As the operation proceeded with time, the fouling behaviors were different between the two systems. Therefore, the model called Hermia used in the study complemented the bench-scale experimental data. However, it could not effectively match the pilot-scale experimental data [223].

Although there are both pilot scale and full-scale demonstrations, but the concept of commercializing PRO still continues to be doubtful. Only Europe's largest renewable energy supplier Statkraft in Norway was the first company to challenge the commercialization process of PRO. Currently PRO process holds significant value as it has high potentials for producing energy for large cities without any pollution. Osmotic power generation is best suited for points where seawater and freshwater come together. This makes most modern cities that are close to rivers highly potential for PRO system setup as it is not theoretically difficult. Besides, the burden of power transportation and power loss reduces when the powerplant is closest to the city. This benefits the system with higher power efficiency and continuous 24 h power supply irrespective to weather restrictions. Moreover, PRO is more convenient and ecologically friendly form of power generation methods when compared to solar and wind power. This is because harmful by-products are not generated in PRO process as it can function without heat emissions or chemical reactions. Other advantages of PRO include the utilization of sewage as the feed solution that are usually generated in the city. This makes PRO even more environmentally friendly and economically sustainable option. To add, PRO can also generate maximum amount of energy even though it is smaller in size compared to other conventional power plants. An estimation from Statkraft's calculations showed that 25 MW of electricity was generated only from a football field-scale osmotic power plant which was able to supply power to almost 30,000 homes [224]. However, the development of novel membrane technologies is required for further improvement in PRO performance since the major cost of PRO comes from the cost of designing and manufacturing membranes. The technological progress will likely alleviate the overall cost and take PRO system towards commercial success.

Currently, no company manufactures commercialization module that has been improved for PRO processes. This is causing disruption in the process of making inexpensive membranes at larger quantities which in turn causing failure to commercially make PRO membranes viable. Also, the development of better membrane will motivate osmotic power generation market to successfully commercialize PRO as companies favors growth in the market for higher R&D and equipment costs to generate appropriate membranes. However, many production companies like Nitto Denko/ Hydranautics, HTI (Hydration Technology Innovations) and Aquaporin have attempted to build PRO commercialized membranes, but they were not effective in commercializing them [225]. Thus, the possibility to increase power density highly depends on the optimization of economic scale of PRO while reducing the cost to generate more power in less area at lower cost. This will result in highly feasible PRO power generation. Likewise, utilizing high salinity brine is expected to be the crucial technology for PRO [225].

More results were obtained from a study by Wan et al., that applied standard Pelton turbines, commercial CTA hollow-fiber membrane modules, and other off-the-shelf components in the process of commercializing PRO process. It was seen from the study that the utilization of off-the-shelf products in other processes instead of being custom-made for the PRO process alone, delivers huge economic advantages. Besides, when the hollow-fiber membrane module was tested utilizing an optimal feed and draw solution, a power density of 24 W/m2 at a pressure of 20 bar was achieved. The power reached up to 38 W/m2 by increasing pressure to 30 bar [226]. This proves that the commercialization of PRO has been advancing to become reality in near future.

To add, the effective operational management is essential for successful commercialization of PRO. As the PRO process comes with few innate membrane problems, it is necessary to know ways to overcome it and organizations interested in the development of PRO power generation must be aware of it as well. Although PRO research appeared to go down due to some recent negative viewpoints towards full-scale PRO process commercialization, but it cannot be denied that that over the last decade, the PRO membranes, its modules, and PRO processes have been considerably improved which adds to its commercialization journey. Yet, translation of PRO membrane to module manufacturing is crucial for the expansion of lab-scale PRO membrane. But, according to modelling studies, these developments in membrane features only have a negligible effect on the overall process performance. Thus, there still remains few uncertainties in manufacturing PRO membrane for PRO commercialization [93].

To summarize, reevaluation of stand-alone PRO processes must be conducted as there still remains few pessimistic prospects of PRO due to thermodynamic energy assessments of the process. As there remains great commercializing potentials for PRO, further clearer comparison along with techno-economic review must be performed for determining the true economic viability of PRO processes.

PRO technology is already leading by mitigating the challenges faced by desalination plants and RO systems related to high energy costs and environmental impacts from brine disposal. PRO process can easily alleviate such problems by treating wastewater and recovering useful energy from it. In fact, when effective cost standards are met, PRO can become cost competitive with other renewable energies which will make the future perspective of the process more durable [227]. Moreover, compared to other salinity energies available in the market, PRO is known to have better efficiency for high density power generation [228]. A recent study by AL Mashrafi et al., demonstrated that PRO process is more efficient when compared to other renewable sources of like wind energy and photovoltaic (PV) system. This is because PRO system can be constructed on non-isolated areas without having a large footprint on that area. PRO system can also be assembled in any location where simply brine and wastewater is generated as it does not depend on climatic conditions like wind and PV technologies [228].

PRO system is free from environmental conditions and does not have any major impacts on nature. Thus, it can be built on areas nearby to cities where electricity is required without causing any harm to the humans [228]. Moreover, considering the shortage in non-renewable resources, the technological advancement and newly emerging trends are focusing more towards harvesting salinity gradient energy with a wide selection of improved approaches. These highly developed methods can not only generate renewable energy but also help in heat recovery, seawater desalination, brine treatment and retrieval of valuable resources [164]. Furthermore, latest studies are mostly targeted towards making PRO functionable on full scale, one of them includes temperature gradient optimization for increasing selectivity and minimizing ICP. This can ultimately increase the power density of technologies like reverse electrodialysis and PRO since both processes are best known for harvesting SGP [229]. These can improve the future perspective of PRO technology by making the system more practical.

Over past decade, PRO process has been aiming towards worldwide commercialization by gradually overcoming its obstacles. Suitable and more realistic prediction of overall costs can be achieved by conducting a complete water quality analysis as well as the long-term effect of fouling. It is expected that with better researchers coming up related to PRO and by overcoming membrane fouling problem, a kickstart of PRO implementation around the world can be accomplished [227]. PRO system highly beneficial as it has a manageable rate of greenhouse gas emission which exceeds wind and solar technologies in terms of environmental sustainability. However, the final decisions must be strongly subjected to cost effectiveness for proper commercialization [228]. Some of this technique's limitation includes production cost of material, process optimization and membrane modification as high-performance membranes are needed for running PRO. From past studies it is evident that membrane module development is crucial for sustainable PRO process [230]. Therefore, membranes must be developed in a way that limits pressure drop, minimizes weak points of the membrane and do not cause any damage to it throughout the operation period [164]. Additionally, the spacers available for PRO cause serious channel blockage and membrane deformation. These spacers are highly unsuitable for PRO operation. Therefore, innovative spacer with advanced performance must be designed for PRO process. Additionally, substitutes of ineffective spaces must be created for purposeful PRO models [231].

A recent economic analysis indicated that the membranes of PRO process takes up >40 % of equipment costs [232]. In this case, a recent study came up with better solution by incorporation of nanofluidic membranes with potentially higher power generation at a lower price rate [233] . This can bring an economic revolution for SGP generation and fast forward the commercialization process of PRO. Different nanomaterials used in PRO process like graphene, GO, and CNT are manufactured using materials that are found abundantly in nature. These materials can be used for large scale membrane modification in a cost-effective manner. From this study results, it is expected that the cost of membrane elements will largely drop in near future resulting in huge market for PRO technology [233]. Moreover, another approach for reducing operational cost of PRO system is by co-locating a desalination plant with wastewater treatment plant. This will eliminate the transmission cost of the PRO system [228].

The implementation of renewable energy is rapidly becoming a crucial factor when resolutions for climate change crisis is discussed. In search of renewable energy options, PRO process can be added to the green energy criteria by successfully reducing CO₂ emission while increasing the global utilization of renewable resources. It can also scale up in terms of easy instalment, minimalism, economic viability, and environmental sustainability [178]. Although many lab-based experiments conducted on PRO process were focused more on enhancing power densities, these setups are irrelevant to the full-scale operation. Therefore, the price, commercial accessibility and large-scale feasible performance of membranes must be taken into consideration. Besides, the permeability of PRO membranes must be checked under full-time pressurization for practical PRO operation [231]. Additionally, future work on PRO must undergo energy analysis and environmental assessments to measure the efficiency of the system, its environmental safety and sustainability [234]. Although there are shortcomings in practicability of PRO as a commercially workable process, but this process yet has an enormous commercial potential. The early system developed by Statkraft went through failure as it had no follow-up after the high-profile Mega-ton and GMVP SWRO-PRO pilot plant projects. This might be proved as disappointing for many researchers but continuous investigations on this project can provide meaningful perception on practical implementation of PRO. Additionally, focusing on the implantation more systemic PRO process design must be applied as the single PRO process has proven difficult to be implemented. Many membrane engineers, academics and scientists are discovering techniques to develop this process and improve its proficiency. In fact, the Middle East is beginning to consider PRO process to enhance the shrinking fresh water source in the region, while producing salinity gradient energy [162]. Furthermore, another improved system that consists of PRO membrane and high brine concentrations from geothermal reservoirs has been developed by SaltPower. This process has been able to successfully create electrical energy through feed water penetrating a selected membrane system and resulting in osmotic power. The membrane was selected as most robust system for the PRO process [235]. Nevertheless, continuous enhancements must be made on PRO system and its membranes to soon make jumpstart of PRO technology worldwide for generating power and proving energy.
